sections.title: SPACEX
sections.summary: Space Exploration Technologies Corp. is conducting an initial public offering of Class A common stock, with plans to list on Nasdaq under the symbol SPCX. The offering includes two classes of stock, with Class B shares having 10 votes per share. Elon Musk will retain significant voting control, making the company a 'controlled company' under Nasdaq rules. The prospectus highlights risks and underwriting details.

sections.title: OUR MISSION

It features scenes like a nighttime rocket launch with exhaust plume, Earth from space with sun rays, an astronaut flying near Earth, a Long March 9 rocket, a spacecraft module, interior views with deployed vehicles, crowds watching a rocket, a structure emitting colored smoke, a jet engine, an astronaut in clouds, a missile launch, and a rocket launch at sunset. sections.title: General Information
sections.summary: Except as otherwise indicated or required by the context, all references to “SpaceX,” the “Company,” “we,” “our” and “us” or similar terms refer to Space Exploration Technologies Corp. and its consolidated subsidiaries. For the definitions of certain terms and abbreviations used in this prospectus, please refer to “Glossary of Terms” beginning on page iv of this prospectus. References to (i) our “bylaws” are to the form of amended and restated bylaws of the Company (as amended and restated from time to time) to be effective upon the completion of this offering, (ii) our “charter” are to the form of restated certificate of formation of the Company to be effective upon the completion of this offering and (iii) “our board” or “the board” are to the board of directors of the Company.

children.title: Basis of Presentation
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/General Information/Basis of Presentation
children.summary: The consolidated financial statements of SpaceX have been retrospectively recast for all periods presented to include (i) the historical results of X.AI Holdings Corp., which was acquired by SpaceX, effective February 2, 2026 (the "xAI Merger"), and X Holdings Corp. ("X Holdings"), which was acquired by xAI, effective March 28, 2025 (the "X Merger"), because these transactions were between entities under common control, and (ii) a five-for-one stock split of the Company's Class A, Class B, and Class C Common Stock, effective May 4, 2026 (the "2026 Stock Split"). Unless otherwise stated or the context otherwise requires, all share and per share information included in this prospectus have been retroactively adjusted to reflect the 2026 Stock Split. Refer to Note 1, Nature of Business, to the audited consolidated financial statements included elsewhere in this prospectus.

sections.title: GLOSSARY OF TERMS
sections.summary: This document provides a comprehensive glossary of terms and abbreviations used throughout a prospectus, covering SpaceX's various business segments including AI (artificial intelligence), Connectivity (Starlink), and Space (Falcon, Dragon, Starship). Key definitions include AI compute, Grok models, Starlink satellite constellations (V1, V2 Mini, V3), launch vehicles (Falcon 9, Falcon Heavy, Starship), and infrastructure like COLOSSUS data centers. It also defines metrics such as ARPU, MAU, and mission success rate, along with technical terms related to satellite orbits, propulsion, and computing.

sections.title: PROSPECTUS SUMMARY
sections.summary: This summary highlights information contained elsewhere in this prospectus. This summary is not complete and does not contain all of the information you should consider before investing in our Class A common stock. You should read this entire prospectus carefully before making an investment decision. You should carefully consider, among other things, the sections titled “Risk Factors,” “Management’s Discussion and Analysis of Financial Condition and Results of Operations,” and our consolidated financial statements and the related notes included elsewhere in this prospectus. Some of the statements in this summary constitute forward-looking statements. Please carefully consider “Cautionary Statement Regarding Forward-Looking Statements.” “You want to wake up in the morning and think the future is going to be great—and that’s what being a space-faring civilization is all about. It’s about believing in the future and thinking that the future will be better than the past. And I can’t think of anything more exciting than going out there and being among the stars.” —Elon Musk

children.title: Our Mission
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/PROSPECTUS SUMMARY/Our Mission
children.summary: Our mission is to build the systems and technologies necessary to make life multiplanetary, to understand the true nature of the universe, and to extend the light of consciousness to the stars. To do this, we have formed the most ambitious, vertically integrated innovation engine on (and off) Earth with unmatched capabilities to rapidly manufacture and launch space-based communications that connect the world, to harness the Sun to power a truth-seeking artificial intelligence that advances scientific discovery, and ultimately to build a base on the Moon and cities on other planets.

children.title: Overview
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/PROSPECTUS SUMMARY/Overview
children.summary: SpaceX is building integrated hardware and software infrastructure across space, connectivity, and AI. It operates Falcon rockets with over 99% mission success, Starlink with ~9,600 satellites serving 10.3 million subscribers across 164 countries, and has acquired xAI to develop Grok, a frontier AI model. The company plans orbital AI compute satellites by 2028. Financial results for Q1 2026 show consolidated revenue of $4,694 million, with Space segment revenue $619 million, Connectivity $3,257 million, and AI $818 million. SpaceX emphasizes vertical integration, first-principles thinking, and truth-seeking AI to enable space exploration and global connectivity.

children.title: Why This Matters Now
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/PROSPECTUS SUMMARY/Why This Matters Now
children.summary: The passage argues that humanity must become a multiplanetary civilization to avoid existential threats and ensure species survival. It highlights the urgency driven by AI's growing energy demands, which strain Earth's resources. The solution is harnessing space-based solar energy, which is far more efficient than terrestrial sources. SpaceX aims to enable this through low-cost space access, satellite manufacturing, and launch capabilities. The ultimate goal is to establish a lunar and Martian presence, leading to a spacefaring civilization that achieves Kardashev Type II status, unlocking unprecedented economic growth and safeguarding humanity's future.

children.title: Who We Are
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/PROSPECTUS SUMMARY/Who We Are
children.summary: SpaceX combines the most transformative and critical technologies in human history, including reusable rockets, a fully global internet service, satellite-to-mobile communications, a real-time information, entertainment and free speech platform, and a truth-seeking AI system designed to accelerate scientific discovery and augment human capabilities.

children.title: Our Unparalleled Launch Capabilities
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/PROSPECTUS SUMMARY/Our Unparalleled Launch Capabilities
children.summary: SpaceX, founded in 2002, has revolutionized space access through reusable rockets, achieving over 650 orbital launches and a 99% mission success rate as of March 2026. Key vehicles include Falcon 9, Falcon Heavy, Dragon, and Starship, with Starship aiming for full reusability and 100 metric tons to orbit. The company also developed Starlink, a global satellite internet constellation, leveraging rapid reusability for cost efficiency and new market opportunities.

children.title: Our Leading Capabilities Across Space, Connectivity, and AI
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/PROSPECTUS SUMMARY/Our Leading Capabilities Across Space, Connectivity, and AI
children.summary: The passage describes SpaceX's three main business segments: Space (launch services with Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy rockets, serving commercial and government customers), Connectivity (Starlink Consumer Broadband, Enterprise Solutions, Government Solutions, and Starlink Mobile, with approximately 9,600 satellites and plans for next-generation V3 satellites), and AI (xAI's vertically integrated platform including compute infrastructure like COLOSSUS, the Grok frontier model, and consumer/enterprise applications like Macrohard).

children.title: Our Repeatable Business Model
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/PROSPECTUS SUMMARY/Our Repeatable Business Model
children.summary: Our business model is built on a repeatable, engineering-driven framework that combines our unparalleled launch capabilities, extreme vertical integration, rapid iteration, and disciplined capital investment to create durable, large-scale businesses. We execute this framework through the following core principles: 1. Leverage our unparalleled launch capabilities to enable massive scale; 2. Identify and create new trillion-dollar market opportunities; 3. Design a solution with world-class engineering and first-principles thinking; 4. Apply “The Algorithm” (make less dumb, delete, optimize, accelerate, automate); 5. Vertically integrate all the way to the end customer; 6. Continuously drive cost down and throughput up; and 7. Generate significant cash flow and reinvest in the future.

children.title: Our Engineering-First Culture
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/PROSPECTUS SUMMARY/Our Engineering-First Culture
children.summary: We are able to achieve transformative technological breakthroughs because we accept only the laws of physics as the limiting factors to our work and mission. Our core approach is deeply rooted in first-principles thinking, which rejects any preconceived notions or experience-based norms. We have a track record of achieving what many have deemed impossible. Some of our industry-defining achievements and historic milestones include: • The first private company to develop and launch a liquid-fuel rocket to reach orbit (2008); • The first private company to successfully dock a private spacecraft with the International Space Station (2012); • The first to successfully propulsively land (2015) and refly orbital-class rocket boosters (2017); • The first to begin deploying a large-scale LEO broadband satellite constellation (2019); - The first private company to transport astronauts to orbit, returning America's ability to fly astronauts to and from the International Space Station (2020); • The first to manufacture consumer-grade phased-array user terminals at scale (2022); • The first to deploy a large-scale LEO satellite-to-mobile constellation (2025); • The first to build a gigawatt-scale AI training cluster and largest coherent supercomputer (2026); • The first gigawatt-scale Megapack battery installation (2026); and • The only company capable of building orbital AI compute at scale.

children.title: Our AI Compute Infrastructure Advantage and Growth Strategy
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/PROSPECTUS SUMMARY/Our AI Compute Infrastructure Advantage and Growth Strategy
children.summary: The passage argues that AI leadership depends on rapidly scaling compute capacity. It highlights a self-reinforcing cycle where lower cost per token improves model quality and user adoption. The main driver of token cost is compute infrastructure efficiency, including hardware and energy. The company claims a dual speed and cost advantage in terrestrial AI with its COLOSSUS clusters, built faster and cheaper than industry benchmarks. It proposes orbital AI compute using SpaceX's launch and satellite expertise to access near-constant solar energy, further reducing token costs. Key enablers include Starship for mass deployment, Starlink's proven satellite technology and mesh network, and the Terafab initiative with Tesla and Intel for chip manufacturing. The company believes its integrated approach across terrestrial and orbital infrastructure provides a distinct advantage for delivering superior, globally accessible AI.

children.title: Our Strengths
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/PROSPECTUS SUMMARY/Our Strengths
children.summary: • Global Leadership in Orbital Launch Services • Unrivaled Satellite and Connectivity Platform across Design, Manufacturing, Deployment, and Operations • Truth-Seeking AI Model Enhanced by Real-Time Data • Extreme Vertical Integration Enabling High Velocity and Superior Cost Efficiency at Scale - Unique Ability to Scale New Trillion-Dollar Markets Across Space, Connectivity, and AI • Business Models that Are Incredibly Difficult to Replicate • Mission-Driven Culture and World-Class Talent

children.title: Future Markets
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/PROSPECTUS SUMMARY/Our Growth Strategies/Future Markets
children.summary: • Point-to-point terrestrial travel - Space tourism • In-orbit manufacturing • Passenger and cargo transport to the Moon and Mars • Energy production on the Moon and Mars • Manufacturing capabilities on the Moon and Mars - Asteroid mining

children.title: Our Market Opportunity
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/PROSPECTUS SUMMARY/Our Market Opportunity
children.summary: We believe we have identified the largest actionable total addressable market (“TAM”) in human history. We estimate that our quantifiable TAM is \$28.5 trillion, consisting of \$370 billion in Space from space-enabled solutions; \$1.6 trillion in Connectivity across \$870 billion in Starlink Broadband and \$740 billion in Starlink Mobile as well as additional opportunities in enterprise and government; \$26.5 trillion in AI across \$2.4 trillion in AI infrastructure, \$760 billion in consumer subscriptions, \$600 billion in digital advertising, and \$22.7 trillion in enterprise applications. For illustrative purposes of sizing our addressable market opportunity, we exclude China and Russia from our global estimates. SpaceX's Estimated TAM by Segment • Manufacturing capabilities on the Moon and Mars <details>0 [images/image-23-Market Opportunity Analysis.jpg] <details> <summary>waterfall</summary> [tables/table-1 Market Data Table.html] SPACE: $370B, CONNECTIVITY: $1.6T, AI: $26.5T </details>

children.title: Our Challenges
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/PROSPECTUS SUMMARY/Our Challenges
children.summary: The passage outlines significant challenges and uncertainties facing the company's growth strategy, which is centered on building, commercializing, and operating services at an unprecedented scale. Key dependencies include the successful development and scaling of Starship and increasing launch cadence, both subject to technical and operational risks. The company's initiatives, such as orbital AI compute, lunar economy, and interplanetary transport, involve unproven technologies and may not achieve commercial viability. Specific challenges for the Space, Connectivity, and AI segments include design modifications, supply chain disruptions, regulatory approvals for spectrum access, and intense competition. These factors could delay execution and negatively impact business and financial results.

children.title: Recent Developments
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/PROSPECTUS SUMMARY/Recent Developments

children.title: Collaboration with Cursor
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/PROSPECTUS SUMMARY/Recent Developments/Collaboration with Cursor
children.summary: In April 2026, xAI entered into a compute and option agreement with Cursor, a private software company. Under the compute agreement, xAI will provide GPU cluster capacity and collaborate on AI models including Grok. The option agreement gives xAI the right to acquire Cursor at a predetermined price. The collaboration aims to integrate AI into developer workflows, leveraging Cursor's coding data for model training. If the option is terminated, Cursor is entitled to a $1.5 billion termination fee and an $8.5 billion deferred services fee, payable in cash or stock.

children.title: Compute Services Agreements with Third Parties
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/PROSPECTUS SUMMARY/Recent Developments/Compute Services Agreements with Third Parties
children.summary: The passage discusses a company's compute infrastructure strategy, highlighting flexibility in allocating capacity for proprietary AI (like Grok 5 at COLOSSUS II) and third-party customers. A key example is a cloud services agreement with Anthropic, paying $1.25 billion per month through May 2029, with termination possible on 90 days' notice. The customer retains IP rights. The company sees this as monetizing unused capacity while supporting internal needs. It also details Elon Musk's significant voting control post-IPO, holding a majority of Class B stock, enabling him to elect directors and control shareholder decisions.

children.title: Our Controlled Company Status
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/PROSPECTUS SUMMARY/Recent Developments/Our Controlled Company Status
children.summary: We will be a controlled company as of the completion of this offering under Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas listing rules. A controlled company is not required to have a majority of its board composed of independent directors or to establish independent compensation and nominating committees. As a controlled company, we will remain subject to rules that require us to have an audit committee composed entirely of independent directors.

children.title: Corporate Information
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/PROSPECTUS SUMMARY/Corporate Information
children.summary: We were founded and incorporated as Space Exploration Technologies Corp., a Delaware corporation, on March 14, 2002 and reincorporated as a Texas corporation on February 14, 2024. Our principal executive offices are located at 1 Rocket Road, Starbase, Texas 78521. Our website address is www.spacex.com. Information contained on our website or linked therein or otherwise connected thereto does not constitute part of nor is it incorporated by reference into this prospectus or the registration statement of which this prospectus forms a part.

children.title: Summary of Risk Factors
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/PROSPECTUS SUMMARY/Summary of Risk Factors
children.summary: The text outlines principal risk factors for investing in Class A common stock, including delays in Starship development, regulatory approvals, satellite connectivity, AI products, and space-related risks. It also covers competition, macroeconomic conditions, capital needs, and governance issues like Elon Musk's control. The offering details include stock structure, outstanding shares, and assumptions for the IPO.

children.title: Summary Historical Consolidated Financial and Operating Data
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/PROSPECTUS SUMMARY/Summary Historical Consolidated Financial and Operating Data
children.summary: The passage presents summary historical consolidated financial and operating data for SpaceX, including statements of operations, cash flows, and pro forma net loss per share. Data is derived from unaudited (for 2026 and 2025 interim periods) and audited (for 2025, 2024, and 2023 annual periods) financial statements. The data reflects the retrospective combination of SpaceX with xAI (acquired February 2, 2026) and X Holdings (acquired March 28, 2025) due to common control. The tables include key financial metrics for the three months ended March 31, 2026 and 2025, and years ended December 31, 2025, 2024, and 2023, with notes on earnings per share and pro forma adjustments for preferred conversion, Class C reclassification, and charter effectiveness.

sections.title: RISK FACTORS_2
sections.summary: Investing in our Class A common stock involves a high degree of risk. You should carefully consider the risks and uncertainties described below, together with all of the other information contained in this prospectus, including our consolidated financial statements and the related notes thereto, before making a decision to invest in our Class A common stock. The disclosures in this section reflect our beliefs and opinions as to factors that could materially and adversely affect us in the future. We may not be able to accurately predict, control, or mitigate these risks. References to past events are provided by way of example only and are not intended to be a complete listing or a representation as to whether or not such factors have or have not occurred in the past or their likelihood of occurring in the future. Additional risks and uncertainties that we are unaware of, or that we currently believe are not material, may also become important factors that adversely affect us. Many of the risks and uncertainties that could materially adversely affect us or our prospects are beyond our control or relate to portions of our business strategy that have a lengthy time horizon or involve unprecedented...to litigation or regulatory scrutiny, which could result in the provision being enjoined or in additional costs or uncertainty. In such case, we may incur additional costs or delays associated with resolving such actions, including in other jurisdictions, which could adversely affect our business, financial condition, or results of operations.

sections.title: CAUTIONARY STATEMENT REGARDING FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS
sections.summary: This prospectus contains forward-looking statements about future operating results, business strategy, and objectives. It covers topics such as Starship development, Starlink satellite deployment, AI platforms, and market growth. These statements are based on current expectations but involve risks and uncertainties, including regulatory approvals, competition, and technological challenges. The company undertakes no obligation to update these statements, and actual results may differ materially.

sections.title: USE OF PROCEEDS
sections.summary: The passage discusses the expected net proceeds from an initial public offering, including the impact of changes in offering price and number of shares sold. The proceeds will be used to fund growth strategies such as AI compute infrastructure, launch infrastructure, launch vehicles, and satellite constellations. Management has flexibility in allocating the funds based on business conditions and cash flows.

sections.title: DIVIDEND POLICY
sections.summary: We do not anticipate declaring or paying any cash dividends to holders of our common stock in the foreseeable future. We currently intend to retain future earnings, if any, to finance the growth of our business. Our future dividend policy is within the discretion of our board and will depend upon then-existing conditions, including our results of operations, financial condition, capital requirements, investment opportunities, statutory restrictions on our ability to pay dividends, restrictions in our existing and any future debt agreements and other factors our board may deem relevant. Covenants under our Credit Agreements also restrict our ability to pay dividends, and we may enter into credit agreements or other borrowing arrangements in the future that restrict our ability to declare or pay cash dividends or make distributions in the future. Please refer to “Management’s Discussion and Analysis of Financial Condition and Results of Operations—Liquidity and Capital Resources” for a description of the restrictions on our ability to pay dividends. Please refer to “Risk Factors—Risks Related to Our Corporate Structure, Ownership of our Class A Common Stock and This Offering—Our ability to provide returns to shareholders will depend on appreciation in our share price, as we do not plan to pay dividends for the foreseeable future.”

sections.title: CAPITALIZATION
sections.summary: The table presents cash, cash equivalents, and capitalization as of March 31, 2026, on actual, pro forma (including Preferred Conversion, Class C Reclassification, and charter effectiveness), and pro forma as adjusted (including the offering and use of proceeds) bases. It references debt agreements: the SpaceX Credit Facility (no borrowings as of April 30, 2026, amended to $5,000 million capacity, due May 2031), the SpaceX Bridge Loan ($20,000 million outstanding as of April 30, 2026, due September 2027), and obligations from AI infrastructure failed sale-leaseback transactions.

sections.title: DILUTION
sections.summary: The passage discusses the immediate and substantial dilution in net tangible book value per share for purchasers of Class A common stock in an offering. It provides calculations of net tangible book value before and after the offering, including adjustments for underwriting discounts, commissions, and the Class C Reclassification and Preferred Conversion. The dilution to new investors is quantified as the difference between the offering price and the adjusted pro forma net tangible book value. A table illustrates per share dilution, and sensitivity analysis shows how changes in offering price or number of shares affect dilution. The summary also includes a table detailing shares acquired, total consideration, and average price per share for existing investors (Mr. Musk and others) and new investors.

sections.title: MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS
sections.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS
sections.summary: The passage discusses the financial condition and results of operations, referencing a consideration paid by shareholders and an assumed initial public offering price. It includes a quote from Elon Musk emphasizing velocity and acceleration as key to technology company competitiveness, stating xAI moves faster than any other company. The management's discussion and analysis section notes that the audited consolidated financial statements reflect retrospective combination of xAI and X Holdings as entities under common control.

children.title: Our Mission
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Our Mission
children.summary: Our mission is to build the systems and technologies necessary to make life multiplanetary, to understand the true nature of the universe, and to extend the light of consciousness to the stars. To do this, we have formed the most ambitious, vertically integrated innovation engine on (and off) Earth with unmatched capabilities to rapidly manufacture and launch space-based communications that connect the world, to harness the Sun to power a truth-seeking artificial intelligence that advances scientific discovery, and ultimately to build a base on the Moon and cities on other planets. Starship Flight Test MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS <details>0 [images/image-28-Rocket Launch.jpg] <details> <summary>natural_image</summary> Rocket launch with visible exhaust plume and flame against cloudy sky (no text or symbols) </details>

children.title: Overview
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Overview
children.summary: SpaceX, founded in 2002, is a vertically integrated company building space, connectivity, and AI infrastructure. It has launched over 80% of global mass to orbit since 2023 with Falcon rockets and operates a Starlink broadband network with ~9,600 satellites across 164 countries. xAI, founded in 2023 and acquired by SpaceX in early 2026, develops the Grok AI model and has built gigawatt-scale compute clusters like COLOSSUS and COLOSSUS II at record speed and low cost. SpaceX revolutionized space access through first-principles thinking and reusability, reducing launch costs by over 85% and aiming for 99% reduction with Starship. The company's integrated approach combines space, AI, and real-time data from X to drive innovation and economic expansion.

children.title: Our Repeatable Business Model
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Our Repeatable Business Model
children.summary: The business model is built on a repeatable, engineering-driven framework combining unparalleled launch capabilities, extreme vertical integration, rapid iteration, and disciplined capital investment. Core principles include leveraging launch capabilities for massive scale, identifying trillion-dollar market opportunities like global broadband and AI infrastructure, applying first-principles engineering, using 'The Algorithm' for iterative improvement, vertically integrating to the end customer, continuously reducing costs and increasing throughput, and reinvesting cash flow into new opportunities.

children.title: Segments in Our Vertically-Integrated Innovation Engine
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Segments in Our Vertically-Integrated Innovation Engine
children.summary: The company reports Q1 2026 consolidated revenue of $4,694 million, operating loss of $(1,943) million, and Adjusted EBITDA of $1,127 million. The Space segment generated $619 million revenue with a $(662) million operating loss, investing $930 million in R&D for Starship. Connectivity segment revenue was $3,257 million with $1,188 million operating income, driven by Starlink growth. The AI segment reported $818 million revenue and a $(2,469) million operating loss, reflecting early-stage investment. Capital expenditures totaled $1,052 million for Space, $1,332 million for Connectivity, and $7,723 million for AI in Q1 2026.

children.title: Space
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Segments in Our Vertically-Integrated Innovation Engine/Space
children.summary: Since our founding in 2002, SpaceX has cracked the code on accessing space at scale, transforming an industry characterized by decades of stagnation, risk aversion, and economically perverse cost structures. We design, manufacture, launch, and refurbish reusable launch vehicles that provide cost-efficient, reliable, and high-cadence access to space for our own purposes as well as for third-party commercial and government customers. In 2025, we launched from four primary launch pads in the United States and successfully recovered boosters across seven landing facilities including autonomous drone ships and catch towers based on the vehicle type and mission profile. Our extensive vertical integration and end-to-end control over the entire value chain, from design to launch to operations, allows us to achieve unprecedented speed and cost efficiency.

children.title: Falcon 9 First Stage Booster Landing
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Segments in Our Vertically-Integrated Innovation Engine/Space/Falcon 9 First Stage Booster Landing
children.summary: The passage details SpaceX's three business segments: Space, Connectivity, and AI. For the three months ended March 31, 2026, capital expenditures were $1,052 million (Space), $1,332 million (Connectivity), and $7,723 million (AI). SpaceX has launched ~7,400 metric tons to orbit with over 99% success, completed ~650 orbital launches, and reduced launch costs significantly. Starlink has ~9,600 satellites, ~10.3 million subscribers, and provides broadband, enterprise, government, and mobile services. The AI segment operates a vertically integrated platform including compute infrastructure, the Grok model, and X platform.

children.title: COLOSSUS II Facility in Memphis, Tennessee
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Segments in Our Vertically-Integrated Innovation Engine/Space/COLOSSUS II Facility in Memphis, Tennessee
children.summary: The passage describes SpaceX's Starlink Mobile satellite-to-mobile connectivity service, partnering with MNOs across 30 countries to reduce dead zones. It also details xAI's AI compute infrastructure, including the COLOSSUS clusters providing 1.0 gigawatt of power, and the development of the Grok frontier model, which has achieved rapid iteration cycles and deep integration with X for real-time data. Additionally, it covers consumer and enterprise applications like Grok Voice and Imagine, and the Macrohard agentic AI platform, with over 1.3 billion supported accounts.

children.title: Our Capital Allocation and Funding Strategy
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Our Capital Allocation and Funding Strategy
children.summary: SpaceX has raised over $9 billion in equity since 2002, building Space and Connectivity segments. Space segment became EBITDA positive in 2018, Connectivity in 2023. In 2025, Space had $653 million Segment Adjusted EBITDA despite $3 billion R&D for Starship; Connectivity earned $7,168 million. Acquired xAI in 2026, planning heavy capital expenditure for AI infrastructure targeting a $26.5 trillion market, funded by debt and equity while maintaining investment grade credit.

children.title: Key Business Metrics
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Key Business Metrics
children.summary: We use the following key business metrics to evaluate our business, measure our performance, identify trends, formulate business plans, and make strategic decisions.

children.title: Space
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Key Business Metrics/Space
children.summary: SpaceX uses mass to orbit and launches as key business metrics. Mass to orbit increased from 1,210 metric tons in 2023 to 2,213 in 2025, and from 450 metric tons in Q1 2025 to 556 in Q1 2026. Falcon 9 launches totaled 96 in 2023, 134 in 2024, and 165 in 2025, with Q1 2026 at 40 launches. Starship is expected to become a larger contributor with future payload capacity of 100 metric tons.

children.title: Connectivity
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Key Business Metrics/Connectivity
children.summary: The text defines Starlink Subscribers as unique Service Lines assigned to Starlink.com accounts without direct negotiated agreements, totaling 10.3 million in Q1 2026 (up 105% YoY) and 5.0 million in Q1 2025 (up 91% YoY). It also defines Starlink Subscriber ARPU as service revenue divided by average subscribers and months, which decreased from $86 to $66 per month between Q1 2025 and Q1 2026, and from $91 to $81 annually from 2024 to 2025, driven by international expansion and lower-priced plans.

children.title: AI
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Key Business Metrics/AI
children.summary: Nameplate Compute Draw: We calculate Nameplate Compute Draw for a period as the number of GPUs installed in our data centers at the end of the period multiplied by their respective all-in power draw. Nameplate Compute Draw reflects installed capacity and does not represent actual power consumption or utilization. It does not include power we install and use for our supporting infrastructure such as cooling systems, power distribution losses, lighting, security systems, or facility-level overhead. Our Nameplate Compute Draw increased to 1.0 gigawatt as of March 31, 2026 as we brought COLOSSUS and COLOSSUS II online. We use this metric to assess our ability to deploy and scale compute capacity. children.title: Segment Income (Loss) from Operations
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Segment Income (Loss) from Operations

children.title: Space Income (Loss) from Operations
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Segment Income (Loss) from Operations/Space Income (Loss) from Operations
children.summary: Space loss from operations for the three months ended March 31, 2026 increased by \$592 million to \$(662) million compared to \$(70) million for the three months ended March 31, 2025, primarily driven by an accelerated investment in development of the Starship vehicle as well as launch facilities to support future Starship launches, and a decrease in revenue from customer launches, partially offset by a decrease in cost of revenue, selling, general, and administrative expenses and impairment. Space income (loss) from operations for the year ended December 31, 2025 decreased by \$678 million to \$(657) million compared to \$21 million for the year ended December 31, 2024, while Space income (loss) from operations for the year ended December 31, 2024 increased by \$22 million to \$21 million for the year ended December 31, 2024 compared to \$(1) million for the year ended December 31, 2023. The year-over-year decrease in 2025 was primarily driven by an accelerated investment in development of the Starship vehicle as well as launch facilities to support future Starship launches, partially offset by an increase in revenue and decrease in cost of revenue.

children.title: Connectivity Income (Loss) from Operations
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Segment Income (Loss) from Operations/Connectivity Income (Loss) from Operations
children.summary: Connectivity income from operations for the three months ended March 31, 2026 increased by \$155 million to \$1,188 million compared to \$1,033 million for the three months ended March 31, 2025, primarily driven by increased revenue from our consumer subscribers (composed of 104.7% growth in Starlink Subscribers, offset by a 22.9% decline in Starlink Subscriber ARPU, primarily due to international expansion and the addition of lower priced service plans) and enterprise business, partially offset by higher depreciation of capitalized launch and satellite costs due to the increase in Starlink flights, as well as higher operating expenses including ground operating costs and international expansion costs to support and drive subscriber growth. Connectivity income from operations for 2025 increased by \$2,417 million to \$4,423 million compared to \$2,006 million for the year ended December 31, 2024 while Connectivity income from operations for the year ended December 31, 2024 increased by \$1,537 million to \$2,006 million compared to \$469 million for the year ended December 31, 2023. The year-over-year increase in 2025 was primarily driven by increased revenue from growth of our consumer and enterprise customers by \$2,378 million and \$1,410 million, respectively, partially offset by higher depreciation of capitalized launch and satellite costs due to the increase in Starlink flights, as well as higher marketing and international expansion costs to drive subscriber growth.

children.title: AI Income (Loss) from Operations
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Segment Income (Loss) from Operations/AI Income (Loss) from Operations
children.summary: AI loss from operations for the three months ended March 31, 2026 increased by \$1,533 million to \$(2,469) million compared to \$(936) million for the three months ended March 31, 2025, primarily driven by higher cloud computing and GPU depreciation costs, data center infrastructure and employee expenses, partially offset by higher revenue. AI loss from operations for 2025 increased by \$4,794 million to \$(6,355) million compared to \$(1,561) million for the year ended December 31, 2024, while AI loss from operations for the year ended December 31, 2024 decreased by \$2,412 million to \$(1,561) million compared to \$(3,973) million for the year ended December 31, 2023. The increase in 2025 was primarily driven by higher cloud computing costs, facilities-related costs and employee expenses, partially offset by higher revenue.

children.title: Segment Adjusted EBITDA
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Segment Adjusted EBITDA
children.summary: Segment Adjusted EBITDA is defined as segment income (loss) from operations excluding (i) depreciation and amortization, (ii) share-based compensation, (iii) restructuring charges and (iv) impairment.

children.title: Space Segment Adjusted EBITDA
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Segment Adjusted EBITDA/Space Segment Adjusted EBITDA
children.summary: Space Segment Adjusted EBITDA for Q1 2026 fell by $575 million to -$351 million, driven by accelerated Starship investment and lower launch revenue. For full year 2025, EBITDA decreased by $501 million to $653 million, while 2024 saw a $157 million increase to $1,154 million. The declines are primarily due to Starship development and launch facility costs, partially offset by NASA CRS missions and a U.S. Department of War contract.

children.title: Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Segment Adjusted EBITDA/Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA
children.summary: Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA for Q1 2026 rose by $469 million to $2,087 million, driven by consumer and enterprise revenue growth. Consumer revenue benefited from 104.7% Starlink subscriber growth, offset by a 22.9% ARPU decline. Enterprise and government revenue increased from aviation, maritime, and mobility growth, partially offset by a government business decline. Higher operating expenses for international expansion and R&D offset revenue gains. For full-year 2025, Adjusted EBITDA increased by $3,319 million to $7,168 million, driven by consumer and enterprise customer growth, offset by higher marketing, expansion costs, and R&D for next-generation products. Strong sequential growth was achieved through expanded LEO satellite constellation scale and efficiency, and verticalized supply chain cost reductions in user terminal production.

children.title: AI Segment Adjusted EBITDA
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Segment Adjusted EBITDA/AI Segment Adjusted EBITDA
children.summary: AI Segment Adjusted EBITDA for the three months ended March 31, 2026 decreased by \$497 million to \$(609) million compared to \$(112) million for the three months ended March 31, 2025, primarily driven by higher cloud compute and data center infrastructure and operating costs, and employee compensation expenses, partially offset by higher revenue. AI Segment Adjusted EBITDA for 2025 decreased by \$1,584 million to \$(1,237) million compared to \$347 million in 2024 while AI Segment Adjusted EBITDA for 2024 decreased by \$875 million to \$347 million, compared to \$1,222 million in 2023. The decrease in 2025 was primarily driven by higher cloud computing costs, facilities-related costs and employee expenses, partially offset by higher revenue. AI Segment Adjusted EBITDA is primarily driven by our strategy to rapidly and cost-effectively scale compute infrastructure. We expect to continue to expand our terrestrial data centers, and to launch orbital data centers, and we expect a multi-year investment horizon before these deployments translate into sustained positive Segment Adjusted EBITDA for our AI segment. Segment Adjusted EBITDA is a non-GAAP measure. Please refer to the section titled “—Non-GAAP Financial Measures” for additional information on our non-GAAP financial measures, including reconciliations of Segment Adjusted EBITDA to segment income (loss) from operations, the most directly comparable GAAP measure.

children.title: Capital Expenditures
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Capital Expenditures
children.summary: The following table presents our capital expenditures by segment: [tables/table-25 Capital Expenditures.html]

children.title: Space Capital Expenditures
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Capital Expenditures/Space Capital Expenditures
children.summary: Space capital expenditures for the three months ended March 31, 2026 increased \$293 million to \$1,052 million compared to \$759 million for the three months ended March 31, 2025. The increase was primarily driven by increased investment in our launch site infrastructure for Starship. Space capital expenditures for 2025 increased \$1,800 million to \$3,832 million compared to \$2,032 million in 2024, while Space capital expenditures for 2024 increased \$535 million to \$2,032 million compared to \$1,497 million in 2023. The increase in each year-over-year period was primarily driven by increased investment in our launch site infrastructure for Starship.

children.title: Connectivity Capital Expenditures
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Capital Expenditures/Connectivity Capital Expenditures
children.summary: Connectivity capital expenditures for the three months ended March 31, 2026 increased \$518 million to \$1,332 million compared to \$814 million for the three months ended March 31, 2025. The increase was primarily driven by higher satellite and ground equipment costs as we continue to increase our number of satellites and grow our satellite network. Connectivity capital expenditures for 2025 increased \$680 million to \$4,178 million compared to \$3,498 million in 2024, while Connectivity capital expenditures for 2024 increased \$1,043 million to \$3,498 million compared to \$2,455 million in 2023. The increase in each year-over-year period was primarily driven by higher satellite and ground equipment costs as we continue to increase our number of satellites and grow our satellite network.

children.title: AI Capital Expenditures
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Capital Expenditures/AI Capital Expenditures
children.summary: AI capital expenditures for the three months ended March 31, 2026 increased \$5,156 million to \$7,723 million compared to \$2,567 million for the three months ended March 31, 2025. The increase was primarily driven by investments in the rapid expansion of our terrestrial data centers, including the development, construction, and equipping of new facilities and supporting infrastructure. AI capital expenditures for 2025 increased \$7,094 million to \$12,727 million compared to \$5,633 million in 2024, while AI capital expenditures for 2024 increased \$5,170 million to \$5,633 million compared to \$463 million in 2023. This increase was primarily driven by significant investments in the rapid expansion of our terrestrial data centers, including the development, construction, and equipping of new facilities and supporting infrastructure.

children.title: Drivers of Our Performance
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Drivers of Our Performance
children.summary: The passage details SpaceX's Starship development, aiming for full reusability to drastically cut launch costs and enable massive payloads (100-200 metric tons). It highlights progress with 11 flight tests and plans for a 12th, focusing on catching the upper stage and in-orbit propellant transfer. Starship is central to deploying next-generation V3 satellites (1 Tbps each) and scaling orbital AI compute, potentially launching 100 GW of compute to space annually. The text also covers Starlink's growth to over 9,600 satellites and 10.3 million subscribers, expansion of satellite-to-mobile connectivity, and vertical integration in AI compute with rapid data center deployment (COLOSSUS II in 91 days). Key themes include reducing launch costs, increasing satellite capacity, scaling AI infrastructure efficiently, and monetizing through advertising, subscriptions, and enterprise/government services.

children.title: Components of Results of Operations
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Components of Results of Operations

children.title: Description of Our Segments
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Components of Results of Operations/Description of Our Segments

children.title: Space
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Components of Results of Operations/Description of Our Segments/Space

children.title: Revenue - Space
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Components of Results of Operations/Description of Our Segments/Space/Revenue - Space
children.summary: Space segment generates revenue from Launch Services (point-in-time recognition) and Launch and Development (over-time recognition). Launch Services uses fixed-price contracts (1-5 years), revenue deferred until payload deployment. Launch and Development uses fixed-price contracts (1-14 years), revenue recognized over time via cost-to-cost method. Internal Starlink launches capitalize costs, no inter-segment revenue. Space revenue growth expected lower than total company growth due to internal absorption of launch capacity.

children.title: Cost of Revenue
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Components of Results of Operations/Description of Our Segments/Space/Cost of Revenue
children.summary: The passage details cost components for SpaceX's Falcon 9, Falcon Heavy, Dragon, and Starship systems. Reusable boosters, fairings, and Merlin engines are classified as property, plant, and equipment and depreciated to cost of revenue. Non-reusable second stages are expensed upon launch. Dragon's reusable capsule is similarly depreciated. Starship, in development, mostly expenses costs as research and development, with Raptor engines expensed during test flights. Space segment cost of revenue includes second stages, launch operations, depreciation, employee compensation, engineering, inventory costs, and shared production costs. This cost is expected to increase in absolute dollars and as a percentage of revenue, varying with the mix of customer and internal launches.

children.title: Research and Development
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Components of Results of Operations/Description of Our Segments/Space/Research and Development
children.summary: Space segment's research and development (“R&D”) expenses mainly relate to the development, build, and testing of Starship. Starship costs consist of test flight hardware, Raptor engines, employee compensation costs (including salaries, benefits, and share-based compensation), tooling and equipment expenses, depreciation for R&D equipment, and allocated overhead. R&D also includes certain expenses related to the development of features and modules created through engineering services for the Company's Falcon vehicles, where the Company retains the associated intellectual property. We expect Space research and development to increase both in absolute dollars and as a percentage of revenue in 2026, as we invest in the development and commercialization of Starship, and to moderate both in absolute dollars and as a percentage of revenue once Starship is commercialized by delivering payload to orbit. At commercialization, Starship costs generally will be capitalized and then depreciated in cost of revenue of the segment associated with the payload delivered.

children.title: Selling, General, and Administrative
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Components of Results of Operations/Description of Our Segments/Space/Selling, General, and Administrative
children.summary: Space segment's selling, general, and administrative (“SG&A”) expenses include allocated employee compensation costs (including salaries, benefits, and share-based compensation) for our sales, facilities, legal, finance, information technology, human resources, and other administrative employees, depreciation, and corporate aircraft costs. We expect Space segment's SG&A to increase in absolute dollars to support growth of our business, and to decrease as a percentage of revenue as we continue to work to reduce operating costs as a percentage of revenue.

children.title: Impairment
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Components of Results of Operations/Description of Our Segments/Space/Impairment
children.summary: Space impairment includes impairment losses on fixed assets due to anomalies on the Company's flight vehicles and launch sites, which occur outside our normal business operations.

children.title: Connectivity
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Components of Results of Operations/Description of Our Segments/Connectivity
children.summary: The Connectivity segment generates revenue from broadband and mobile connectivity services via Starlink, and from sales of the Starlink Kit. Service revenue is recognized over time as customers receive benefits, either ratably for fixed-price monthly subscriptions or based on actual data consumption for variable-price services. Broadband contracts are month-to-month, while mobile agreements are multi-year but terminable at any time. For multi-year Starshield contracts, revenue is recognized over time using the cost-to-cost input method. Starlink Kit revenue is recorded upon delivery or installation, net of returns and credits.

children.title: Expenses - Connectivity
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Components of Results of Operations/Description of Our Segments/Connectivity/Expenses - Connectivity

children.title: Cost of Revenue
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Components of Results of Operations/Description of Our Segments/Connectivity/Expenses - Connectivity/Cost of Revenue
children.summary: Connectivity segment's cost of revenue includes depreciation (inclusive of launch, satellite, and ground infrastructure costs), Starlink Kit costs, shipping and handling costs, ground operating expenses, employee compensation costs (including salaries, benefits, and share-based compensation) for our engineering and operations teams, payment processor fees, warranty expense, inventory excess and obsolescence, and customs and duties. We expect Connectivity cost of revenue to increase in absolute dollars as we grow our revenue, and to decrease as a percentage of revenue as we continue to drive efficiencies in our next-generation satellites, Starlink Kits, and ground infrastructure.

children.title: Research and Development
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Components of Results of Operations/Description of Our Segments/Connectivity/Expenses - Connectivity/Research and Development
children.summary: Connectivity segment's R&D expenses mainly relate to the development, build, and testing of our next-generation satellites, Starlink Kits, and ground infrastructure. These costs include employee compensation costs (including salaries, benefits, and share-based compensation), contractor compensation expenses, equipment lease expenses, depreciation for R&D equipment, and allocated overhead. We expect Connectivity research and development to increase in absolute dollars as we grow our revenue, and to decrease as a percentage of revenue as we scale our business.

children.title: Selling, General, and Administrative
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Components of Results of Operations/Description of Our Segments/Connectivity/Expenses - Connectivity/Selling, General, and Administrative
children.summary: Connectivity segment's SG&A expenses include allocated employee compensation costs (including salaries, benefits, and share-based compensation) for our sales, facilities, legal, finance, information technology, human resources, and other administrative employees, licensing and regulatory fees, marketing expenses, depreciation, and bad debt expense. We expect Connectivity SG&A to increase in absolute dollars and as a percentage of revenue in 2026 as we introduce marketing spend to support growth of our business, and to decrease as a percentage of revenue over time as we continue to work to reduce operating costs as a percentage of revenue.

children.title: Impairment
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Components of Results of Operations/Description of Our Segments/Connectivity/Expenses - Connectivity/Impairment
children.summary: Connectivity impairment includes costs related to discontinuation of a product line for Starlink Kits that is non-recurring.

children.title: AI
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Components of Results of Operations/Description of Our Segments/AI
children.summary: The AI segment generates revenue from digital platform services including advertising on X, and AI solutions such as subscriptions, data licensing, and API access to Grok models. Advertising revenue is recognized upon ad engagement, with seasonality peaking in Q4. Subscription and licensing revenues are recognized ratably over contract terms ranging from month-to-month to two years.

children.title: Cost of Revenue
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Components of Results of Operations/Description of Our Segments/AI/Cost of Revenue
children.summary: AI segment's cost of revenue includes infrastructure costs, revenue share expenses, payment processor fees, payments to creators, amortization of acquired intangible assets, and allocated labor and overhead costs. Infrastructure costs consist primarily of costs related to data center facilities, including lease and hosting costs, related support, maintenance, energy, and bandwidth costs, depreciation of servers and networking equipment, public cloud hosting costs, and employee compensation costs (including salaries, benefits, and share-based compensation) for our operations teams. We expect AI cost of revenue to increase in absolute dollars as we grow our revenue, and to decrease as a percentage of revenue as we monetize our products and as we expand our service offerings for AI solutions.

children.title: Research and Development
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Components of Results of Operations/Description of Our Segments/AI/Research and Development
children.summary: AI segment's R&D expenses mainly relate to the training of Grok, our leading frontier model, development, build, and testing of our next-generation AI-enabled products and data center costs to train AI-enabled products. These costs include cloud computing expenses, employee compensation expenses (including salaries, benefits, and share-based compensation), power generation costs, and depreciation of data center assets, including processors, equipment lease expenses, and networking equipment. We expect AI R&D expenses to increase, both in absolute dollars and as a percentage of revenue, as we invest in compute infrastructure for Grok. Additionally, AI R&D expenses may increase as a result of the compute agreement with Cursor.

children.title: Selling, General, and Administrative
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Components of Results of Operations/Description of Our Segments/AI/Selling, General, and Administrative
children.summary: AI segment's SG&A expenses consist primarily of employee compensation expenses (including salaries, benefits, and share-based compensation) for our sales, sales support, marketing, finance, legal, information technology, human resources and other administrative employees. In addition, SG&A expenses include fees and costs for professional services, including consulting, content moderation, third-party legal and accounting services and facilities costs and other supporting overhead costs that are not allocated to other departments. We expect AI SG&A to increase in absolute dollars to support growth of our business, and to decrease as a percentage of revenue as we continue to work to reduce operating costs as a percentage of revenue. Additionally, AI SG&A may increase as a result of the compute agreement with Cursor.

children.summary: AI restructuring charges are the result of the acquisition of Twitter in October 2022 by X Holdings. The charges include workforce restructuring for former Twitter employees, as well as impairment and early termination penalties as a result of consolidation of Twitter's various office leases.

children.summary: The provision for (benefit from) income taxes consists primarily of income taxes in certain federal, state, local and foreign jurisdictions in which we conduct business. Foreign jurisdictions typically have different statutory tax rates from those in the United States. Accordingly, our effective tax rates may vary depending on the impact of the valuation allowance as well as the relative proportion of foreign income to domestic income, generation of tax credits, changes in the valuation of our deferred tax assets and liabilities, and changes in tax laws. children.summary: Revenue for the year ended December 31, 2025 increased by \$290 million, or 7.6%, compared to the prior year ended December 31, 2024. Launch Services revenue remained relatively flat year over year, while Launch and Development revenue increased by \$298 million. The Launch and Development revenue increase was primarily driven by increased revenue for an extended contract with NASA for additional Cargo Resupply Services (CRS) missions to the International Space Station and increased revenue from a U.S. Department of War contract. While total Falcon launches increased by 31 from 134 in 2024 to 165 in 2025, Space customer launches and average price per launch remained relatively flat year over year.

children.title: Cost of Revenue
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Comparison of the Years Ended December 31, 2025 and 2024/Segment Results/Cost of Revenue
children.summary: Cost of revenue for the year ended December 31, 2025 decreased by \$189 million, or 12.2%, compared to the prior year ended December 31, 2024. This decrease was primarily due increased reusability of our Falcon launch vehicles resulting in lower depreciation of \$240 million, lowering the cost of each launch, and lower overhead costs of \$11 million. The decrease is also due to the relative increase in Starlink satellite launches from 89 launches in 2024 to 122 launches in 2025, resulting in relatively more of our launch operations and overhead costs capitalized in our Connectivity segment of \$14 million. This decrease was partially offset by an increase in inventory excess and obsolescence reserves of \$51 million mainly due to less demand on rocket vehicle and spacecraft parts as reusability has increased.

children.title: Research and Development
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS/Comparison of the Years Ended December 31, 2025 and 2024/Segment Results/Research and Development
children.summary: Research and development for the year ended December 31, 2025 increased by \$1,169 million, or 63.7%, compared to the prior year ended December 31, 2024. This increase was primarily driven by higher production costs of \$779 million, higher launch costs of \$218 million, and higher engineering costs of \$185 million, due to the accelerated investment in development of the Starship vehicle and continued development of production and launch facilities to support future Starship launches.

children.summary: Selling, general, and administrative for the year ended December 31, 2025 decreased by \$26 million, or 6.9%, compared to the prior year ended December 31, 2024. This decrease was primarily due to lower allocated general and administrative overhead of \$52 million, partially offset by higher employee compensation expenses (including salaries, benefits, and share-based compensation) of \$16 million.

children.summary: Revenue for the year ended December 31, 2025 increased by \$3,788 million, or 49.8%, compared to the prior year ended December 31, 2024. This increase was primarily driven by an increase of \$2,377 million in revenue from our consumer subscribers, composed of 99.9% growth in Starlink Subscribers, offset by an 11.2% decline in Starlink Subscriber ARPU, primarily due to international expansion and the addition of lower priced service plans. In addition, Connectivity revenue had an increase of \$1,411 million from our enterprise and government customers, primarily driven by the growth in our enterprise connectivity business of \$1,218 million inclusive of growth in our mobile connectivity business of \$632 million, and growth in our government connectivity business of \$193 million.

This decrease was due to the impairment on the office leases assumed as part of the Twitter acquisition that primarily occurred during the year ended December 31, 2023, partially offset by an increase in workforce-related restructuring charges.
