These stations transmit data between satellites and terrestrial internet networks. We operate ground stations around the world, with over 400 sites globally.

children.title: Customer Case Studies
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/THE ALGORITHM 01 MAKE THE REQUIREMENTS/Customer Case Studies
children.summary: The following examples illustrate ways in which customers across a range of industries have used and benefited from our solutions within our Space, Connectivity, and AI segments. These examples are intended to highlight representative applications of our offerings and the types of operational, performance and efficiency benefits that customers may realize. In addition, we include examples of our deployment of Starlink services in response to natural disasters, which demonstrate our ability to rapidly establish communications infrastructure to support emergency response and recovery efforts in challenging environments. </details> <details>0 [images/image-90-Rocket Launch.jpg] <details> <summary>natural_image</summary> Rocket launch from a coastal platform with visible exhaust plume and launch tower (no text or symbols on the main image) </details>

children.title: BACKGROUND
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/THE ALGORITHM 01 MAKE THE REQUIREMENTS/Customer Case Studies/BACKGROUND
children.summary: SES is a premier global communications satellite operator managing an extensive fleet of geostationary (GEO) and medium Earth orbit (MEO) satellites. As the satellite industry became increasingly capital-intensive and competitive, SES identified a critical need for high-cadence, cost-effective, and reliable access to orbit to maintain its fleet and drive down costs.

children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/THE ALGORITHM 01 MAKE THE REQUIREMENTS/Customer Case Studies/KEY BENEFITS
children.summary: 01 Early adopter of flight-proven booster technology, helping validate reusability

children.title: OUR SOLUTION
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/THE ALGORITHM 01 MAKE THE REQUIREMENTS/Customer Case Studies/OUR SOLUTION
children.summary: SES is a prominent commercial partner of SpaceX. In 2013, SES became the first commercial entity to launch a satellite on a Falcon 9 into geostationary transfer orbit. This partnership reached a milestone in 2017, when SES became the first company to launch a commercial satellite on a flight-proven rocket booster, validating SpaceX's reusability model for the global aerospace industry. Overall, SES and SpaceX have completed 19 successful missions together and counting delivering both SES and Intelsat spacecraft to orbit. 02 Accelerated time-to-orbit with a 100% mission success rate ### KEY BENEFITS <details>0 [images/image-91-United In-Flight Entertainment.jpg] <details> <summary>natural_image</summary> Person holding smartphone displaying a video game on a digital screen, with 'UNITED' logo visible in background (no readable text on screen or device) </details> HIGHLIGHTS [tables/table-53 Starlink Aviation.html] KEY BENEFITS [tables/table-54 Starlink Benefits.html]

children.title: BACKGROUND_2
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/THE ALGORITHM 01 MAKE THE REQUIREMENTS/Customer Case Studies/BACKGROUND_2
children.summary: United is the world's largest airline and is the leading carrier across the Atlantic and Pacific. Historically, inflight Wi-Fi has fallen short of customer expectations at every airline and become a pain point. United recognized that Starlink delivers fast, reliable internet access around the world — including over oceans, polar regions, and other remote locations previously unreachable by traditional cell or Wi-Fi signals — and saw this collaboration as a key way to differentiate itself from its competitors to drive customer satisfaction, trust, and loyalty. United is rolling out Starlink at an unprecedented speed and scale. The average Starlink installation takes about 8 hours — roughly 10 times faster than non-Starlink equipment. The airline expects three out of every four departures to have Starlink Wi-Fi by the end of 2026 and to have the service installed on all its dual-cabin aircraft by the end of 2027. "Thanks to Starlink, everything you can do on the ground, you can do onboard a United plane at 35,000 feet, just about anywhere in the world."

children.title: Scott Kirby, United Chief Executive Officer
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/THE ALGORITHM 01 MAKE THE REQUIREMENTS/Customer Case Studies/Scott Kirby, United Chief Executive Officer

children.title: OUR SOLUTION_2
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/THE ALGORITHM 01 MAKE THE REQUIREMENTS/Customer Case Studies/OUR SOLUTION_2
children.summary: United Airlines partnered with SpaceX to deploy Starlink Wi-Fi across its fleet, offering free, fast, gate-to-gate connectivity. The service enables live TV, streaming, and more on personal devices and seatback screens. Installation is 10 times faster than traditional equipment, and United aims to have Starlink on three-quarters of departures by 2026 and all dual-cabin aircraft by 2027. Customer satisfaction scores have doubled on regional flights.

Starlink is a superior technology at sea providing better coverage, faster performance, and lower operational costs." Jason Liberty, Royal Caribbean Group Chairman & Chief Executive Officer

children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/THE ALGORITHM 01 MAKE THE REQUIREMENTS/Customer Case Studies/KEY BENEFITS_2
children.summary: 01 Industry-leading connectivity for land-like experiences, including streaming and lag-free video calls 02 Allows a real-time digital ecosystem to function for crew operations as well as guest onboard booking through the mobile app

children.title: BACKGROUND_3
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/THE ALGORITHM 01 MAKE THE REQUIREMENTS/Customer Case Studies/BACKGROUND_3
children.summary: Royal Caribbean Group operates a global fleet of 69 ships calling on over 1,000 destinations across all seven continents. Historically, maritime internet was one of the industry's primary friction points for travelers, relying on legacy satellites that suffered from high latency, limited bandwidth, and frequent signal drops in remote polar or mid-ocean regions. As guest expectations shifted toward high-bandwidth activities like live streaming, video conferencing, and online gaming, Royal Caribbean identified a critical need for a consistent and reliable experience across its entire global footprint.

children.title: OUR SOLUTION_3
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/THE ALGORITHM 01 MAKE THE REQUIREMENTS/Customer Case Studies/OUR SOLUTION_3
children.summary: In 2022, Royal Caribbean Group became the first cruise company to sign a fleet-wide agreement with SpaceX for Starlink, using low-Earth orbit satellites to provide high-speed, low-latency connectivity at lower cost than traditional satellite service. In 2025, a Starlink Community Gateway on the Star of the Seas achieved 10 Gbps symmetrical throughput, the fastest satellite internet on a commercial vessel. This enables seamless streaming and work for thousands of guests and reliable crew connectivity, eliminating dead zones in remote areas like the Arctic and Antarctic.

The SATCOM solution unlocks the John Deere tech stack so every farmer can fully utilize their current precision agriculture technology in addition to the new innovative solutions they will deploy in the future." Jahmy Hindman, Senior Vice President & Chief Technology Officer

children.title: BACKGROUND_4
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/THE ALGORITHM 01 MAKE THE REQUIREMENTS/Customer Case Studies/BACKGROUND_4
children.summary: John Deere is a global leader in agricultural machinery that increasingly relies on technology to deliver precision agriculture and autonomous solutions to farmers. Despite high demand for these digital tools, approximately 70% of agricultural land in Brazil and 30% in the United States lack sufficient terrestrial cellular coverage. This connectivity gap prevents farmers from utilizing critical advancements like real-time data sharing and autonomous equipment operations. To overcome this challenge, Deere required a robust communication solution capable of delivering reliable connectivity in the most remote environments to ensure its technology-enabled products could deliver value to farmers.

children.title: OUR SOLUTION_4
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/THE ALGORITHM 01 MAKE THE REQUIREMENTS/Customer Case Studies/OUR SOLUTION_4
children.summary: In January 2024, John Deere entered into an industry-first collaboration with SpaceX to bring reliable connectivity to its machines. By making Starlink a standard offering, communication gaps are bypassed and precision agriculture is enabled at scale. Deere can now support high-bandwidth applications like remote diagnostics and autonomous navigation in regions previously plagued by poor connectivity. Delivering Starlink-equipped machines in Brazil was a priority due to lack of reliable coverage in key rural and agricultural regions. Beyond Brazil, Starlink is connecting Deere machines in the U.S., Australia, Canada, New Zealand, with plans for further expansion. The entire installation — including a ruggedized Starlink terminal, a 4G LTE modem, and connection to the John Deere Operations Center — takes less than two hours. 03 Industry-leading performance with speeds up to 120 Mbps and low 25 ms latency 04 Reduced machine downtime through remote diagnostics and enhanced self-repair solutions ### OUR SOLUTION <details>0 [images/image-95-Flexport Cargo Plane.jpg] <details> <summary>text_image</summary> flexport Sponsored By: ATLAS AIR INC. flexport </details>

Importantly here, we're doing live translation using AI. So you can actually just speak in your language, they will see it in their language." Ryan Petersen, Founder & CEO

children.title: KEY BENEFITS_4
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/THE ALGORITHM 01 MAKE THE REQUIREMENTS/Customer Case Studies/KEY BENEFITS_4
children.summary: 01 Eliminates "English-only" requirement for trade, enabling seamless native-language coordination 02 Reduces potential manual coordination errors by localizing all notifications and views 03 Removes coordination lag inherent in cross-border trade via real-time localization and native-language messaging 04 Transcends the limitations of legacy translation tools by providing context-aware, in-app translations for high-stakes logistics data

children.title: BACKGROUND_5
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/THE ALGORITHM 01 MAKE THE REQUIREMENTS/Customer Case Studies/BACKGROUND_5
children.summary: For the last 200 years, English has functioned as the mandatory global language for trade, creating a significant barrier to entry and a constant source of operational friction for non-English speakers. In the complex world of global logistics, miscommunications in data or shipment instructions can lead to costly delays, compliance errors, and stranded inventory. Flexport, which serves over 13,000 customers across 135 countries, identified a critical need to democratize access to the supply chain by allowing every participant to communicate natively without sacrificing technical precision. " [Translations] make it easier than ever to actually get global trade done." Mike Leskinen, CFO

children.title: OUR SOLUTION_5
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/THE ALGORITHM 01 MAKE THE REQUIREMENTS/Customer Case Studies/OUR SOLUTION_5
children.summary: In 2026, Flexport integrated Grok directly into its core platform to power Flexport Translations, including AI Live Message Translation and AI Platform Translation. With AI Live Message Translation, Grok moves beyond the limitations of legacy translation tools and provides context-aware, in-app translations that allow importers, exporters, and truckers to message in their native tongues while recipients see the text instantly in theirs. Grok also powers AI Platform Translation, which localizes the entire Flexport dashboard and notification system. This ensures that global teams can work independently and operate with fewer manual errors. Through Grok, Flexport offers support for English, Mandarin (Simplified and Traditional), Vietnamese, Thai, Korean, Italian, German, and Spanish. Mike Leskinen, CFO <details>0 [images/image-96-Hurricane Aftermath.jpg] <details> <summary>natural_image</summary> Aerial view of a devastated urban area with collapsed buildings and debris, no visible text or symbols. </details>

children.title: BACKGROUND_6
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/THE ALGORITHM 01 MAKE THE REQUIREMENTS/Customer Case Studies/BACKGROUND_6
children.summary: Hurricane Helene made landfall in northwest Florida as a Category 4 storm on September 26, 2024, followed shortly by Hurricane Milton on October 9, which made landfall along Florida's west-central Gulf Coast as a Category 3 storm. SpaceX delivered fast, reliable internet access in areas where traditional networks were down or non-existent. Ground-based Starlink devices that connect with Starlink satellites were deployed to provide internet services to remote areas affected by the hurricanes. SpaceX provided free service for the rest of the year to help with response and recovery.

children.title: CALIFORNIA WILDFIRES
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/THE ALGORITHM 01 MAKE THE REQUIREMENTS/Customer Case Studies/CALIFORNIA WILDFIRES

children.title: BACKGROUND_8
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/THE ALGORITHM 01 MAKE THE REQUIREMENTS/Customer Case Studies/BACKGROUND_8
children.summary: In January 2025, several destructive wildfires erupted across Los Angeles County, causing widespread electrical failures and disrupting traditional cellular networks for thousands of residents. To mitigate the loss of communication, SpaceX and T-Mobile activated the direct-to-cell Starlink system on an emergency basis, utilizing a constellation of satellites to provide connectivity. SpaceX donated over 1,000 terminals of Starlink hardware to response agencies conducting rescue and relief efforts and provided one month of free service to residents in the most severely impacted areas to assist with long-term recovery efforts. ### CALIFORNIA WILDFIRES <details>0 [images/image-98-车载天线.jpg] <details> <summary>natural_image</summary> Exterior view of a car rooftop with solar panels and a damaged building under a cloudy sky (no signage or text visible) </details> When Hurricane Melissa made landfall in Jamaica as a record-breaking Category 5 hurricane, the storm had devastating effects across the island. Starlink provided 1,000+ terminals to response agencies conducting rescue and relief operations, free service to over 21,000 residential customers affected by the hurricane, and facilitated the transmission of over two million SMS messages through Starlink Mobile and our work with local telecommunications providers.

children.title: Competition
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/THE ALGORITHM 01 MAKE THE REQUIREMENTS/Competition
children.summary: SpaceX faces competition in three main segments: Space (launch services), Connectivity (satellite broadband), and AI (AI models and cloud). In Space, competitors include United Launch Alliance, Arianespace, Northrop Grumman, Blue Origin, and Rocket Lab. SpaceX holds advantages in launch breadth and cadence. In Connectivity, Starlink competes with terrestrial providers like Verizon and Comcast, and satellite operators like EchoStar and Amazon LEO. In AI, competitors include OpenAI, Google, and Meta. All markets have high barriers to entry such as capital, technology, and regulatory approvals.

children.title: Intellectual Property
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/THE ALGORITHM 01 MAKE THE REQUIREMENTS/Intellectual Property
children.summary: The passage describes the company's intellectual property strategy, which includes proprietary knowledge, software, brands, and selectively patented inventions. Protection relies on patents, trademarks, trade secrets, copyrights, confidentiality agreements, and contractual commitments. The company has filed for U.S. and international protections and may license rights to or from others. It also uses confidentiality and invention assignment agreements with employees and contractors.

children.title: Human Capital
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/THE ALGORITHM 01 MAKE THE REQUIREMENTS/Human Capital
children.summary: As of March 31, 2026, we employed over 22,000 full-time employees worldwide, none of whom are subject to any collective bargaining agreement. We believe our strong culture of collaboration and innovation distinguishes us and serves as an important driver of our business performance.

children.title: Regulatory Environment
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/THE ALGORITHM 01 MAKE THE REQUIREMENTS/Regulatory Environment
children.summary: The passage discusses the company's need to comply with various governmental regulations that impact capital expenditures, earnings, and competitive position. It highlights dependencies on maintaining authorizations for launches, satellite operations, and U.S. government programs, as well as compliance with evolving AI, data privacy, and cybersecurity laws. The company incurs substantial costs to meet requirements from agencies like the FAA and FCC, and faces risks from export controls and sanctions. Becoming a public company adds further legal and accounting expenses.

children.title: Space
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/THE ALGORITHM 01 MAKE THE REQUIREMENTS/Regulatory Environment/Space
children.summary: The Space segment is heavily regulated in the U.S. and internationally. Key regulations include FAA licenses for commercial launches and reentries, FCC licenses for radio communications and spacecraft operations (including orbital debris mitigation), and U.S. export/import controls. As a U.S. government contractor, it must comply with the Federal Acquisition Regulation, security clearances, and CFIUS reviews for foreign investments. Delays in regulatory approvals can disrupt operations.

children.title: Connectivity
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/THE ALGORITHM 01 MAKE THE REQUIREMENTS/Regulatory Environment/Connectivity
children.summary: The passage discusses the critical role of FCC authorizations and spectrum licenses for SpaceX's satellite connectivity services, including Starlink Mobile. It highlights that without these licenses, services cannot be offered in markets. In the U.S., authorizations involve FCC approvals for satellite systems, earth stations, and spectrum use, subject to technical and operational conditions. Internationally, coordination through the ITU is required. Licenses typically last 10-15 years and must be renewed; revocation or non-renewal would halt services. State regulations are generally preempted by federal law but may impose reporting, safety, and consumer protection requirements.

children.title: AI
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/THE ALGORITHM 01 MAKE THE REQUIREMENTS/Regulatory Environment/AI
children.summary: The passage discusses evolving AI laws and regulations across jurisdictions, imposing requirements on development, deployment, and use of AI systems, including security, transparency, and export controls. It highlights risks from intellectual property claims, misuse, and emerging legislation like the EU AI Act, California SB 53, and New York RAISE Act. These create compliance uncertainty, operational complexity, and potential legal liability, requiring investment in mitigation measures.

children.title: Privacy, Cybersecurity, Data Protection, Online Safety, and Digital Platform Regulation
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/THE ALGORITHM 01 MAKE THE REQUIREMENTS/Regulatory Environment/Privacy, Cybersecurity, Data Protection, Online Safety, and Digital Platform Regulation
children.summary: The passage discusses complex global legal and regulatory frameworks related to privacy, cybersecurity, AI, data protection, and digital platforms. It highlights challenges from evolving laws like the California Consumer Privacy Act and GDPR, as well as government contractor requirements such as the Department of War's Cybersecurity Maturity Model Certification. The text covers risks from cross-border data transfer restrictions, online safety laws (e.g., UK Online Safety Act), and cybersecurity threats from sophisticated actors. It notes that compliance is costly and operationally demanding, potentially requiring product changes and increasing operational complexity.

children.title: Environmental, Health, and Safety
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/THE ALGORITHM 01 MAKE THE REQUIREMENTS/Regulatory Environment/Environmental, Health, and Safety
children.summary: The passage discusses the extensive regulatory framework governing operations, including environmental, health, and safety laws at federal, state, local, and foreign levels. It covers permits for construction and operation, such as zoning, air quality, and discharge permits, and notes potential costs and limitations from environmental reviews and mitigation measures.

children.title: Government Contracts
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/THE ALGORITHM 01 MAKE THE REQUIREMENTS/Regulatory Environment/Government Contracts
children.summary: The passage describes the company's revenue dependence on U.S. government contracts, primarily with NASA, the Department of War, and intelligence agencies, for launch services, spacecraft development, and AI products. These are firm fixed-price contracts with milestone payments, subject to FAR and DFARS regulations, which impose stringent compliance requirements and risks such as unilateral termination, scope reduction, audits, and security clearance revocation. The company must also meet cybersecurity standards (NIST SP 800-171) and report cyber incidents. Additionally, significant capital improvements at government-owned launch sites are transferred to the government upon lease expiration.

children.title: Legal Proceedings
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/THE ALGORITHM 01 MAKE THE REQUIREMENTS/Legal Proceedings
children.summary: We are involved in the legal proceedings described in Note 17, Commitments and Contingencies, in our audited consolidated financial statements and Note 16, Commitments and Contingencies in our unaudited consolidated financial statements included elsewhere in this prospectus, and we are subject to other claims and litigation arising in the ordinary course of business. The outcome of any litigation is inherently uncertain, and if decided adversely to us, or if we determine that settlement of particular litigation is appropriate, we may be subject to liability that could have a material adverse effect on our business.

sections.title: MANAGEMENT
sections.summary: Below is certain information as of May 1, 2026 regarding individuals who are expected to serve as our executive officers and directors upon the completion of this offering. [tables/table-55 SpaceX Executives.html]

children.title: Executive Officers and Management Directors
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT/Executive Officers and Management Directors
children.summary: The text provides biographical details for three key executives of a company. Elon Musk is the CEO, CTO, and Chairman since 2002, with roles at Tesla, xAI, Neuralink, and The Boring Company, and a background in physics and business. Gwynne Shotwell is President and COO since 2008, with prior aerospace experience and degrees in mechanical engineering and applied mathematics. Bret Johnsen is CFO since 2011, with financial leadership at Mindspeed and Broadcom, holding a B.S. in Accounting and an M.S. in Finance, and is a CPA.

children.title: Non-Management Directors
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT/Non-Management Directors
children.summary: The text profiles six board members of a company: Ira Ehrenpreis (DBL Partners, Tesla board), Randy Glein (DFJ Growth), Antonio J. Gracias (Valor Management, Neuralink, The Boring Company), Donald Harrison (Google), Steve Jurvetson (Future Ventures, Draper Fisher Jurvetson), and Luke Nosek (Gigafund, Founders Fund, PayPal co-founder). Each brings experience in venture capital, technology, corporate governance, and leadership across various industries.

children.title: Additional Information
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT/Additional Information
children.summary: The passage details two legal actions involving Elon Musk. First, a 2018 SEC settlement over false tweets about taking Tesla private, resulting in a $20 million fine, a three-year ban as Tesla chairman, and pre-clearance procedures for public statements about Tesla, later amended in 2019. Second, a 2026 partial judgment in Pampena v. Musk in the Northern District of California, where a jury found Musk violated securities laws for two statements in May 2022 regarding his potential purchase of Twitter, while clearing him on other claims. Musk has filed post-trial motions challenging the judgment, with hearings expected in June 2026.

children.title: Family Relationships
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT/Family Relationships
children.summary: There are no family relationships among any of our directors or executive officers.

children.title: Controlled Company Exemption
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT/Controlled Company Exemption
children.summary: Upon completion of the offering, Mr. Musk will own approximately 1/2% of Class B common stock, entitling him to elect 51% of directors and hold 1/2% of total voting power. This makes the company a 'controlled company' under Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas rules, allowing exemptions from certain governance requirements such as independent director majority, independent nominating and compensation committees, and annual performance evaluations. The company intends to use these exemptions, reducing protections for Class A shareholders. If it ceases to be a controlled company, it must comply with all governance requirements within transition periods.

children.title: Composition of Our Board
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT/Composition of Our Board
children.summary: Upon the consummation of the offering, our board will consist of eight directors. Subject to the terms of our charter and bylaws, the number of directors on our board will be determined from time to time by our board. Under the terms of our charter, the holders of our outstanding Class B common stock, voting separately as a class, will have the right to elect 51% of the total number of authorized directors, rounded up to the nearest whole number (the “Class B Directors”). Holders of Class A and Class B common stock, voting together as a single class, will elect the remaining members of our board (the “Common Stock Directors”). We expect that upon the completion of the offering Mr. Musk, Gwynne Shotwell, Antonio J. Gracias, Donald Harrison, and Luke Nosek will serve as the initial Class B Directors and Ira Ehrenpreis, Randy Glein, and Steve Jurvetson will serve as the initial Common Stock Directors. Our board will be subject to annual elections. Each director will hold office until the next annual meeting of our shareholders and until his or her successor is duly elected and qualified or until his or her earlier death, resignation or removal (as provided in our charter). For additional information, please refer to “Description of Capital Stock.”

children.title: Role of our Board in Risk Oversight
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT/Role of our Board in Risk Oversight
children.summary: We face a number of risks, including those described under the section titled “Risk Factors” included elsewhere in this prospectus. Our board believes that risk management is an important part of establishing, updating and executing on our business strategy. Our board, as a whole and at the committee level, has oversight responsibility relating to risks that could affect our corporate strategy, business objectives, compliance, operations and financial condition and performance. Our board focuses its oversight on the most significant risks facing us and on the processes to identify, prioritize, assess, manage and mitigate those risks. While our board has an oversight role, management is principally tasked with direct responsibility for management and assessment of risks and the implementation of processes and controls to mitigate their effects on us.

children.title: Director Independence
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT/Director Independence
children.summary: Based upon information requested from and provided by each director concerning his or her background, employment and affiliations, our board has determined that each of Ira Ehrenpreis, Randy Glein, Donald Harrison, Steve Jurvetson, and Luke Nosek is independent within the meaning of the listing standards of Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas currently in effect. In making this determination, our board considered the relationships that each of these directors has with our company and all other facts and circumstances our board deemed relevant in determining their independence, including (i) the beneficial ownership of our capital stock by each such director and/or investment funds or other entities affiliated with them and (ii) the relationships set forth below under “Certain Relationships and Related Person Transactions.” The board also considered that Donald Harrison was employed by an organization that does business with Company. The amount received by the Company or such other organization in each of the last three fiscal years did not exceed the greater of \$200,000 or 5% of either the Company’s or such organization’s consolidated gross revenues.

children.title: Board Leadership Structure
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT/Board Leadership Structure
children.summary: Upon the completion of this offering, as provided in our charter, our board will continue to be led by Mr. Musk. Pursuant to the terms of our charter, he can only be removed from the board and these leadership positions by the affirmative vote of the holders of a majority of the outstanding shares of our Class B common stock, voting separately as a class. Our board has concluded that our current leadership structure is appropriate at this time.

children.title: Board Committees
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT/Board Committees
children.summary: In connection with the completion of this offering, our board will establish an audit committee and a compensation and nominating committee. Audit and compensation and nominating committees will be governed by their charters that will be available on our website at www.spacex.com. Pursuant to our bylaws, our board may, from time to time, establish other committees to facilitate the management of our business and operations. Information contained on our website or linked therein or otherwise connected thereto does not constitute part of nor is it incorporated by reference into this prospectus or the registration statement of which this prospectus forms a part.

children.title: Audit Committee
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT/Board Committees/Audit Committee
children.summary: The audit committee assists the board in overseeing financial statement integrity, legal compliance, and auditor independence. It prepares SEC reports, approves audit and non-audit services, and may retain advisors. After the offering, Randy Glein and Steve Jurvetson will serve, with Glein as chair and expected audit committee financial expert. A third member will be appointed within one year.

children.title: Compensation and Nominating Committee
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT/Board Committees/Compensation and Nominating Committee
children.summary: The compensation and nominating committee oversees the company's compensation philosophy, reviews and approves executive officer compensation, recommends independent director compensation, and advises on board composition. After the offering, Ira Ehrenpreis, Antonio J. Gracias, and Luke Nosek will serve on the committee. As a controlled company, it relies on an exemption from Nasdaq independence requirements. Ehrenpreis and Nosek qualify as independent directors, with Ehrenpreis expected to chair the committee.

children.title: Compensation Committee Interlocks and Insider Participation
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT/Compensation Committee Interlocks and Insider Participation
children.summary: During the last completed fiscal year, we were not a publicly traded company and did not have a compensation committee or any other committee serving a similar function. Historically, the board has been responsible for determining, and has made all decisions regarding, the compensation for Mr. Musk. With respect to those expected to serve as our other executive officers, Mr. Musk has had primary responsibility for compensation-related decisions; however, all equity awards were approved by the board.

children.title: Code of Business Conduct and Ethics
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT/Code of Business Conduct and Ethics
children.summary: In connection with this offering, our board will adopt a code of business conduct and ethics applicable to our employees, directors and officers, in accordance with applicable SEC rules and the corporate governance rules of Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas. We expect that any amendments to the code or any waivers of its requirements applicable to our directors and executive officers will be disclosed on our website at www.spacex.com, as and to the extent required by applicable SEC rules and the corporate governance rules of Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas. Information contained on our website or linked therein or otherwise connected thereto does not constitute part of, nor is it incorporated by reference into, this prospectus or the registration statement of which this prospectus forms a part.

children.title: Corporate Governance Guidelines
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/MANAGEMENT/Corporate Governance Guidelines
children.summary: In connection with the completion of this offering, we intend to adopt corporate governance guidelines, which will set forth expectations for directors, director qualification standards, committee structure and functions and other policies for the governance of our company. A copy of our corporate governance guidelines will be posted on our website at www.spacex.com. Information contained on our website or linked therein or otherwise connected thereto does not constitute part of, nor is it incorporated by reference into, this prospectus or the registration statement of which this prospectus forms a part.

children.title: Compensation Discussion and Analysis
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis
children.summary: This Compensation Discussion and Analysis, or CD&A, provides an overview of our executive compensation philosophy, objectives, and design and each element of our executive compensation program with regard to the compensation awarded, to, earned by, or paid to the following named executive officers (collectively, our “NEOs”) for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2025 (the “2025 Fiscal Year”), which includes all of our executive officers for the 2025 Fiscal Year. For the 2025 Fiscal Year, our NEOs were: [tables/table-56 SpaceX Executives.html]

children.title: Our Compensation Philosophy and Objectives
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Our Compensation Philosophy and Objectives
children.summary: Our compensation program is designed to attract, retain and reward executives and employees, with a heavy emphasis on equity compensation to provide employees with a financial stake in our business and an ownership mindset. We offer a number of programs that allow employees to voluntarily elect to receive elements of their compensation in equity or to otherwise increase their ownership interests in the Company.

children.title: Process for Setting Compensation
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Process for Setting Compensation
children.summary: Historically, our board has been responsible for determining, and has made all decisions regarding, the compensation for Mr. Musk. With respect to the other NEOs, Mr. Musk has had primary responsibility for compensation-related decisions (in consultation with Ms. Shotwell with respect to Mr. Johnsen's compensation). All equity awards are approved by our board. In connection with this offering, we plan to establish a compensation and nominating committee of our board who will oversee our executive compensation program going forward. The compensation and nominating committee, in consultation with Mr. Musk (other than with respect to his own compensation), will have primary responsibility for evaluating and approving the compensation of our NEOs or making recommendations regarding such compensation to our board when appropriate, including with respect to Mr. Musk's compensation.

children.title: Elements of Compensation
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Elements of Compensation

children.title: Base Salary
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Elements of Compensation/Base Salary
children.summary: Each NEO’s base salary is a fixed component of compensation for performing specific job duties and functions. Base salaries are generally reviewed on an annual basis, taking into account the NEO’s experience and responsibilities. Mr. Musk’s base salary of \$54,080 has remained unchanged since 2019, and prior to our relocation to Texas in 2024 was tied to California’s minimum salary for exempt employees. Mr. Musk has historically determined the base salary for Ms. Shotwell, which was increased from \$1,040,000 to \$1,080,000 effective April 20, 2025. Mr. Musk and Ms. Shotwell have historically determined the base salary for Mr. Johnsen, which was increased from \$780,000 to \$825,000 on April 6, 2025, with retroactive effect for the full 2025 Fiscal Year. As participants in a broader employee equity election program, our NEOs, other than Mr. Musk, were eligible to elect to receive all or a portion of their base salary in the form of restricted stock units (“RSUs”). For the 2025 Fiscal Year, Ms. Shotwell received \$353,077 of her base salary in cash and the remainder as a grant of 19,650 RSUs that vested 50% on May 15, 2025 and 50% on November 15, 2025, and Mr. Johnsen elected to receive his base salary fully in cash. The base salaries paid to our NEOs reflect the only cash compensation that they are eligible to receive, as no NEO participates in an annual bonus program.

children.title: Long-Term Incentive Compensation
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Elements of Compensation/Long-Term Incentive Compensation
children.summary: In 2025, the company granted long-term incentives under the 2024 Equity Incentive Plan, replacing the 2015 Plan. The 2024 Plan authorizes up to 365,950,000 shares of Class C common stock for stock options, RSUs, and other awards. Outstanding awards under both plans will convert to Class A common stock on a one-for-one basis. Elon Musk received no annual long-term incentive due to his ownership but was granted a performance-based award in January 2026. Ms. Shotwell received 27,030 RSUs and stock options for 324,325 shares as part of her target award, plus special stock options for 3,537,740 shares. Mr. Johnsen received stock options for 324,325 shares and special stock options for 141,510 shares, with vesting schedules tied to continued employment.

children.title: Other Elements of Compensation
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Other Elements of Compensation

children.title: Retirement Benefits
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Other Elements of Compensation/Retirement Benefits
children.summary: All of our U.S. employees, including our NEOs, are eligible to participate in our 401(k) plan, which is a broad-based, tax-qualified defined contribution retirement plan. Under the 401(k) plan, we may make discretionary matching and non-elective contributions, subject to certain limits under the Internal Revenue Code of 1986, as amended (the “Code”), and such contributions would vest ratably and would be 100% vested after five years of credited service; however, no such company contributions were made for 2025.

children.title: Employee Stock Purchase Plans
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Other Elements of Compensation/Employee Stock Purchase Plans
children.summary: Historically, we have provided two employee stock purchase plans in which all of our U.S. employees, including the NEOs, are eligible to participate. Our Amended and Restated 2017 Employee Stock Purchase Plan (the “2017 ESPP”) is intended to qualify under Section 423 of the Code and allows eligible employees to purchase shares of Class C common stock using accumulated payroll contributions at a discount. It is expected that the 2017 ESPP will be amended and restated in connection with this offering, as described below. Our 2023 Non-Qualified ESPP (the “NQ ESPP”) is not intended to qualify under Section 423 of the Code and allows eligible employees to purchase shares of Class C common stock using accumulated payroll contributions at fair market value. Our NQ ESPP will be discontinued in connection with this offering.

children.title: Perquisites
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Other Elements of Compensation/Perquisites
children.summary: The Company provides security equipment to enhance security at Ms. Shotwell's personal residence. The aggregate incremental cost of these security benefits are reported in the “—Executive Compensation Tables—2025 Summary Compensation Table” below. No other material perquisites are provided to our NEOs.

children.title: Other Matters
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Other Matters
children.summary: The text details 2026 compensation developments for Mr. Musk and Mr. Johnsen. For Musk, the board approved 1 billion performance-based restricted shares of Class B common stock vesting upon achieving 15 market capitalization milestones and establishing a permanent Mars colony with at least one million inhabitants. After the xAI Merger, these milestones were equitably adjusted. Additionally, a performance stock award from xAI was assumed and later replaced with 302,072,285 performance-based restricted shares vesting upon 12 market cap milestones (from $1.065 trillion to $6.565 trillion) and completing non-Earth-based data centers delivering 100 terawatts of compute per year. For Johnsen, an amendment to 4 million performance-based stock options changed vesting to 371,125 options per $10 billion in adjusted EBITDA achieved annually from 2025 to 2029, with a one-year and one-day service requirement after earning. No options were earned for 2025.

children.title: Clawback Policy
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Clawback Policy
children.summary: In connection with this offering, we will adopt a compensation recoupment (clawback) policy that complies with the Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas listing standards implementing Rule 10D-1 of the Exchange Act.

children.title: Executive Compensation Tables
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Executive Compensation Tables
children.summary: The 2025 Summary Compensation Table details total compensation for Named Executive Officers (NEOs) in the 2025 Fiscal Year, including salary, option awards, and stock awards (RSUs) with grant date fair values calculated per FASB ASC Topic 718. Additional notes cover security costs for Ms. Shotwell, personal travel on company aircraft, and RSUs granted in lieu of base salary.

children.title: Grants of Plan-Based Awards
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Executive Compensation Tables/Grants of Plan-Based Awards
children.summary: The table details stock options and RSUs granted to NEOs in fiscal 2025 under the 2024 Plan, excluding Mr. Musk. It includes grant dates, exercise prices based on fair market value, and grant date fair values per FASB ASC Topic 718. Notably, Ms. Shotwell received RSUs in lieu of $726,923 of her base salary.

children.title: Outstanding Equity Awards at Fiscal Year-End
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Executive Compensation Tables/Outstanding Equity Awards at Fiscal Year-End
children.summary: The table details outstanding stock option awards for NEOs as of December 31, 2025, with no RSUs or other unvested awards. Options for Class C common stock will convert to Class A on a one-for-one basis. Vesting schedules vary: some options vest in monthly installments through 2026-2031, others have milestone-based vesting (e.g., cost reduction targets or free cash flow performance), and some have specific tranche vesting dates. All vesting is subject to continued employment.

children.title: Option Exercises and Stock Vested
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Executive Compensation Tables/Option Exercises and Stock Vested
children.summary: The following table reflects stock options to purchase Class C common stock exercised by our NEOs during the 2025 Fiscal Years and RSUs held by our NEOs which vested during 2025. [tables/table-61 Executive Awards.html] (1) The value realized on the exercise of stock options is determined based on the fair market value of a share of our Class C common stock on the exercise date, less the applicable exercise price. (2) The value realized on the vesting of RSUs is determined based on the fair market value of a share of our Class C common stock on the vesting date.

children.title: Potential Payments Upon Termination or Change in Control
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Potential Payments Upon Termination or Change in Control
children.summary: None of our NEOs are party to an employment agreement or severance arrangement that provides for payments or benefits upon termination of employment or a change in control of the Company. Under the terms of the RSU award agreements, in the event of an NEO's death, the RSUs scheduled to vest within the following 12-month period would become vested. No NEOs held outstanding RSUs as of December 31, 2025. No other equity award agreements provide for benefits upon termination of employment or a change in control of the Company.

children.title: Amended and Restated 2024 Equity Incentive Plan
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Amended and Restated 2024 Equity Incentive Plan
children.summary: In connection with this offering, we intend to amend and restate our 2024 Plan (the “A&R 2024 Plan”). The purpose of the A&R 2024 Plan is to secure and retain the services of eligible employees, directors and consultants to provide incentives for such persons to exert maximum efforts for the success of the Company and to provide a means by which such eligible recipients may be given an opportunity to benefit from increases in value of our Class A common stock. The A&R 2024 Plan allows for the grant of stock options, both incentive stock options and “nonstatutory” stock options; stock appreciation rights (“SARs”); restricted stock; RSUs; and other equity awards. We refer to these collectively herein as “Awards.” The following description of the A&R 2024 Plan is not intended to be complete and is qualified in its entirety by reference to the complete text of the A&R 2024 Plan, a copy of which will be filed as an exhibit to the registration statement of which this prospectus forms a part. Please read the A&R 2024 Plan in its entirety.

children.title: Administration
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Amended and Restated 2024 Equity Incentive Plan/Administration
children.summary: The A&R 2024 Plan will be administered by our board or a committee thereof designated by our board to administer the A&R 2024 Plan, which we refer to herein as the “Plan Administrator.” The Plan Administrator will have broad authority, subject to the provisions of the A&R 2024 Plan, to administer and interpret the A&R 2024 Plan and Awards granted thereunder. All decisions and actions of the Plan Administrator will be final, binding and conclusive on all persons.

children.title: Stock Subject to A&R 2024 Plan
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Amended and Restated 2024 Equity Incentive Plan/Stock Subject to A&R 2024 Plan
children.summary: The maximum number of shares of Class A common stock that may be issued under the A&R 2024 Plan will not exceed 365,950,000 shares (the “Share Reserve”), inclusive of shares issued under the 2024 Plan prior to the adoption of the A&R 2024 Plan. The Share Reserve is subject to certain adjustments in the event of a change in our capitalization. Shares of Class A common stock issued under the A&R 2024 Plan may be authorized but unissued or reacquired shares, including shares repurchased by the Company on the open market or otherwise. Shares of Class A common stock subject to any award under our 2012 Equity Incentive Plan or the 2015 Plan that expires, terminates or is forfeited or that are reacquired, withheld or not issued to satisfy a tax withholding obligation will be added to the Share Reserve. Shares of Class A common stock subject to any award under the A&R 2024 Plan that expires, terminates or is forfeited or that are reacquired, withheld or not issued to satisfy a tax withholding obligation or payment of an exercise price will be again be available for issuance under the A&R 2024 Plan.

children.title: Eligibility
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Amended and Restated 2024 Equity Incentive Plan/Eligibility
children.summary: Current or prospective employees, non-employee directors and consultants of the Company and its affiliates will be eligible to participate in the A&R 2024 Plan.

children.title: Types of Awards
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Amended and Restated 2024 Equity Incentive Plan/Types of Awards
children.summary: The text describes various equity awards under the A&R 2024 Plan, including stock options (incentive and nonstatutory), stock appreciation rights (SARs), restricted stock, RSUs, and other equity awards. Key details include exercise prices based on fair market value, maximum terms of 10 years, and payment methods such as cash, stock, or broker commitments. The Plan Administrator determines vesting conditions and restrictions.

children.title: Performance Criteria
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Amended and Restated 2024 Equity Incentive Plan/Performance Criteria
children.summary: The Plan Administrator may specify certain performance criteria which must be satisfied before Awards will be granted or will vest. The performance goals may vary from participant to participant, group to group, and period to period.

children.title: Transferability
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Amended and Restated 2024 Equity Incentive Plan/Transferability
children.summary: Except as otherwise permitted by the Plan Administrator, Awards generally are not transferable except by will or by the laws of descent and distribution, and each stock option or SAR will be exercisable during the lifetime of the participant only by the participant.

children.title: Clawback
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Amended and Restated 2024 Equity Incentive Plan/Clawback
children.summary: Awards will be subject to recoupment in accordance with any clawback policy that we adopt, including any clawback policy required under Rule 10D-1 of the Exchange Act.

children.title: Amendment and Termination
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Amended and Restated 2024 Equity Incentive Plan/Amendment and Termination
children.summary: The Plan Administrator may amend, suspend or terminate the A&R 2024 Plan at any time; however certain enumerated material amendments may not be made without shareholder approval. Suspension or termination of the A&R 2024 Plan may not impair the rights and obligations of any outstanding Award. The Plan Administrator may also amend any outstanding Award, subject to the participant's consent in the event such amendment impairs such participant's rights under such Award. The A&R 2024 Plan is expected to be adopted by our board in connection with this offering and will terminate on December 10, 2034, unless earlier terminated by our board.

children.title: Second Amended and Restated 2017 Employee Stock Purchase Plan
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Second Amended and Restated 2017 Employee Stock Purchase Plan
children.summary: In connection with this offering, we intend to further amend and restated our 2017 ESPP. The purpose of the A&R 2017 ESPP is to encourage and enable our eligible employees to acquire a proprietary interest in us through the ownership of our Class A common stock. The A&R 2017 ESPP, and the rights of participants to make purchases thereunder, is intended to qualify under the provisions of Section 423 of the Code. The following description of the A&R 2017 ESPP is not intended to be complete and is qualified in its entirety by reference to the complete text of the A&R 2017 ESPP, a copy of which will be filed as an exhibit to the registration statement of which this prospectus forms a part. Please read the A&R 2017 ESPP in its entirety.

children.title: Administration
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Second Amended and Restated 2017 Employee Stock Purchase Plan/Administration
children.summary: The A&R 2017 ESPP will be administered by our board or a committee thereof designated by our board to administer the A&R 2017 ESPP, which we refer to herein as the “ESPP Administrator.” The ESPP Administrator has the final power to determine all questions of policy and expediency that may arise in the administration of the A&R 2017 ESPP. The ESPP Administrator may delegate its responsibilities under the A&R 2017 ESPP to one or more other persons.

children.title: Stock Subject to A&R 2017 ESPP
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Second Amended and Restated 2017 Employee Stock Purchase Plan/Stock Subject to A&R 2017 ESPP
children.summary: The maximum number of shares of Class A common stock that may be issued under the A&R 2017 ESPP will not exceed 75,000,000 shares (the “ESPP Share Pool”), inclusive of shares issued under the 2017 ESPP prior to the adoption of the A&R 2017 Plan. The ESPP Share Pool is subject to certain adjustments in the event of a change in our capitalization. Shares of Class A common stock issued under the A&R 2017 ESPP may be either authorized and unissued shares or previously issued shares acquired by us. A participant does not have the rights of a shareholder until the shares are actually issued to the participant.

children.title: Eligibility; Limitations
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Second Amended and Restated 2017 Employee Stock Purchase Plan/Eligibility; Limitations
children.summary: An employee is eligible to participate in the A&R 2017 ESPP if the employee has been continuously employed by us our one of our related corporations incorporated in the United States since at least the last day of the calendar month preceding the month in which the offering date occurs and does not own 5% or more of the combined voting power of the Company or any related corporations (as determined under Section 423 and 424 of the Code). Eligible employees must enroll in a particular offering at least 10 business days prior to the offering date of such offering, and once enrolled for an offering, employees will be automatically enrolled in subsequent offerings unless the employee withdraws. A participant is not permitted to purchase shares of our Class A common stock with a fair market value in excess of \$25,000 in any one calendar year (calculated based on the fair market value on the offering date).

children.title: Offerings
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Second Amended and Restated 2017 Employee Stock Purchase Plan/Offerings
children.summary: The ESPP Administrator determines offerings and purchase periods under Section 423 of the Code, with expected six-month successive periods ending April 15 and October 15. Participants contribute 1% to 100% of eligible earnings via payroll deductions, changeable before an offering but only decreased up to twice during it (second to 0%). At period end, deductions buy Class A common stock at the purchase price; remaining funds carry over. Termination or withdrawal before ten business days before purchase date returns accumulated deductions.

children.title: Purchase Price
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Second Amended and Restated 2017 Employee Stock Purchase Plan/Purchase Price
children.summary: The price per share at which shares are purchased under the A&R 2017 ESPP in a particular offering period is determined by the ESPP Administrator, but in no event will be less than 85% of the lower of the fair market value of the Class A common stock on the offering date or the fair market value of the Class A common stock on the purchase date.

children.title: Adjustments
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Second Amended and Restated 2017 Employee Stock Purchase Plan/Adjustments
children.summary: In the event of any reorganizations, recapitalizations, stock splits, reverse stock splits, stock dividends, extraordinary dividends or distributions, or similar events, the ESPP Administrator will appropriately adjust the number and class of shares available under the A&R 2017 ESPP and subject to the purchase limits under each ongoing offering and the applicable purchase price of such shares in each ongoing offering.

children.title: Transferability
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Second Amended and Restated 2017 Employee Stock Purchase Plan/Transferability
children.summary: Rights to purchase Class A common stock under the A&R 2017 ESPP may not be transferred by a participant and may be exercised during a participant's lifetime only by the participant.

children.title: Amendment and Termination
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Compensation Discussion and Analysis/Second Amended and Restated 2017 Employee Stock Purchase Plan/Amendment and Termination
children.summary: The A&R 2017 ESPP will become effective when it is approved by our board. Our board may amend, alter, or discontinue the A&R 2017 ESPP in any respect at any time, subject to shareholder approval as required by applicable laws and regulations.

children.title: Director Compensation
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION/Director Compensation
children.summary: During 2025, our non-employee directors did not receive cash or equity compensation for their service on our board. Mr. Musk and Ms. Shotwell do not receive any additional compensation for their respective services as directors.

sections.title: CERTAIN RELATIONSHIPS AND RELATED PERSON TRANSACTIONS
sections.path: spacex-s1.pdf/CERTAIN RELATIONSHIPS AND RELATED PERSON TRANSACTIONS
sections.summary: The following is a description of certain relationships and transactions that exist, are proposed to exist or have existed or that we have entered into or propose to enter into with our directors, executive officers, holders of more than 5% of our capital stock or their affiliates and immediate family members since January 1, 2023 and where: • we have been or are to be a participant; - the amount involved exceeded or will exceed \$120,000; and - any of our directors, executive officers, or holders of more than 5% of our capital stock, or any immediate family member of, or persons sharing their household with, any of these individuals, had or will have a direct or indirect material interest.

children.title: Note Regarding the xAI Merger
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/CERTAIN RELATIONSHIPS AND RELATED PERSON TRANSACTIONS/Note Regarding the xAI Merger
children.summary: On February 2, 2026, we effected the xAI Merger, pursuant to which we acquired xAI (which includes X). For the purposes of the disclosures set forth in this section pursuant to Item 404 of Regulation S-K, the transactions described below also include certain agreements and transactions originally entered into by xAI or X Holdings prior to the xAI Merger to the extent that such agreements and transactions are ongoing following the consummation of the xAI Merger.

children.title: Transactions with Elon Musk and Affiliated Entities
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/CERTAIN RELATIONSHIPS AND RELATED PERSON TRANSACTIONS/Transactions with Elon Musk and Affiliated Entities
children.summary: Elon Musk, our founder, Chief Executive Officer, Chief Technical Officer, Chairman of our board, and principal shareholder, also serves as the Technoking, Chief Executive Officer and director of Tesla, and is an approximately 20% shareholder of Tesla as of November 10, 2025. Mr. Musk is also the founder of several other ventures, including The Boring Company (an infrastructure company). In addition, Mr. Musk was a stockholder, director, and officer of each of xAI and X prior to the X Merger and the xAI Merger. We have certain relationships and/or transactions with Mr. Musk and affiliated entities, as described below.

children.title: Transactions with Tesla
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/CERTAIN RELATIONSHIPS AND RELATED PERSON TRANSACTIONS/Transactions with Tesla
children.summary: Tesla holds a beneficial ownership of 18,990,195 shares of Class A common stock (less than 1%). The passage details various commercial agreements between Tesla and other Musk-affiliated entities. SpaceX obtained goods/services from Tesla worth $11M (2023), $4M (2024), $144M (2025), and $0.2M (early 2026). xAI obtained goods/services from Tesla worth $191M (2024), $506M (2025), and $34M (early 2026), while recognizing $2M (2025) and $0.4M (early 2026) in revenue from Tesla. Tesla purchased advertising on X platform totaling $0.5M (2024), $4M (2025), and $0 (early 2026). SpaceX charged Tesla $1M (2023), $1M (2024), $2M (2025), and $0 (early 2026) for aircraft usage.

children.title: Transactions with The Boring Company
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/CERTAIN RELATIONSHIPS AND RELATED PERSON TRANSACTIONS/Transactions with The Boring Company
children.summary: In 2024, X entered into a lease for office space with a subsidiary owned by The Boring Company (an entity affiliated with Mr. Musk). Under this agreement, X made lease payments of \$0.1 million in 2024, \$1 million in 2025, and \$0.1 million from January 1, 2026 through February 28, 2026. In addition, SpaceX incurred expenses of \$1 million in 2025 in connection with the construction of tunnels by The Boring Company in Bastrop, Texas.

children.title: Relationships with Musk Industries LLC
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/CERTAIN RELATIONSHIPS AND RELATED PERSON TRANSACTIONS/Relationships with Musk Industries LLC
children.summary: xAI leases a real property owned by the Musk Industries LLC, which is owned by Mr. Musk. Under this agreement, xAI made lease payments of \$0.5 million in 2024, \$2 million in 2025, and \$0.2 million from January 1, 2026 through February 28, 2026.

children.title: Security Services provided to Mr. Musk
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/CERTAIN RELATIONSHIPS AND RELATED PERSON TRANSACTIONS/Security Services provided to Mr. Musk
children.summary: We are party to a services agreement with a security company owned by Mr. Musk and organized to provide security services concerning him, including in connection with his duties to and work for SpaceX. SpaceX incurred expenses of \$2 million for such SpaceX-related security services in 2023, \$3 million for such security services in 2024, \$4 million for such security services in 2025, and \$1 million for such security services from January 1, 2026 through February 28, 2026.

children.title: Relationship with Antonio J. Gracias and Affiliated Entities
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/CERTAIN RELATIONSHIPS AND RELATED PERSON TRANSACTIONS/Relationship with Antonio J. Gracias and Affiliated Entities

children.title: Transactions with Valor Equity Partners and Affiliated Entities
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/CERTAIN RELATIONSHIPS AND RELATED PERSON TRANSACTIONS/Transactions with Valor Equity Partners and Affiliated Entities
children.summary: xAI subsidiaries have equipment lease agreements with Valor Equity Partners, involving aggregate payments of $6,986 million, $6,633 million, and $6,587 million. Payments of $885 million in 2025 and $857 million through February 2026 have been made. X also received payments from Valor for API services: $1 million in 2024, $1 million in 2025, and $0.1 million through February 2026.

children.title: Other Transactions with our Directors and Executive Officers
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/CERTAIN RELATIONSHIPS AND RELATED PERSON TRANSACTIONS/Other Transactions with our Directors and Executive Officers
children.summary: SpaceX discloses expenses for aircraft used by directors and executives. Through Falcon Landing, LLC, it operates three aircraft, with maintenance costs for one serviced by an affiliate of Mr. Musk totaling $1M (2023), $1M (2024), $3M (2025), and $1M (Jan-Feb 2026). Tesla is invoiced for use of SpaceX's aircraft. When SpaceX aircraft are unavailable, Mr. Musk uses his personal plane and is reimbursed, incurring expenses of $0.1M (2023), $3M (2024), $2M (2025), and $0.2M (Jan-Feb 2026). Ms. Shotwell and Mr. Johnsen co-own an aircraft; when SpaceX aircraft are unavailable, it is used for business with waived leasing fees, and SpaceX covers maintenance, crew, and operation costs, totaling $3M (2023), $3M (2024), $3M (2025), and $1M (Jan-Feb 2026).

children.title: Investors' Rights Agreement
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/CERTAIN RELATIONSHIPS AND RELATED PERSON TRANSACTIONS/Investors' Rights Agreement
children.summary: Certain existing investors in our equity securities, including entities affiliated with Elon Musk, Google, Valor, and DFJ Growth, are party to an Amended and Restated Investors' Rights Agreement, dated as of August 4, 2020 (the "Investors' Rights Agreement"). Under the Investors' Rights Agreement, such existing investors are entitled to registration rights with respect to shares of our Class A common stock beneficially owned by them (collectively, the "Registrable Securities"). These registration rights, if exercised, would require us to register such existing investors' Registrable Securities under the Securities Act, and would facilitate the resale of such securities by such existing investors into the public markets. We will pay all registration expenses, other than underwriting discounts and commissions, associated with registrations effected pursuant to the Investors' Rights Agreement, subject to limited exceptions.

children.title: Demand Registration Rights
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/CERTAIN RELATIONSHIPS AND RELATED PERSON TRANSACTIONS/Investors' Rights Agreement/Demand Registration Rights
children.summary: At any time commencing six months after the effective date of the first registration statement for a public offering of our securities (other than a registration on certain registration forms or for transactions not providing for the sale of Registrable Securities), such holders of a majority of the then-outstanding Registrable Securities, excluding for this purpose shares issuable or issued upon conversion of certain series of our preferred stock, may request that we file a registration statement within 60 days after receipt of the request covering the offer and sale of Registrable Securities, provided that, among other things, the anticipated aggregate offering price, net of underwriting discounts and selling expenses, exceeds \$250.0 million. Further, the initiating holders may require that such registration be an underwritten offering, in which case the underwriter will be selected by a majority in interest of the initiating holders, subject to our reasonable approval.

children.title: Piggyback Registration Rights
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/CERTAIN RELATIONSHIPS AND RELATED PERSON TRANSACTIONS/Investors' Rights Agreement/Piggyback Registration Rights
children.summary: If we propose to register any of our securities under the Securities Act for sale to the public for cash (other than on certain registration forms or for transactions that do not permit piggyback participation), we must promptly give each holder of Registrable Securities notice of such proposed registration and, upon timely request, cause to be registered all Registrable Securities that such holder requests to be included, subject to any cutbacks, as permitted by the agreement.

children.title: Policies and Procedures for Review of Related Person Transactions
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/CERTAIN RELATIONSHIPS AND RELATED PERSON TRANSACTIONS/Policies and Procedures for Review of Related Person Transactions
children.summary: The passage describes a written policy adopted by the company for reviewing and approving related person transactions. The audit committee will review transactions exceeding $120,000 involving directors, executive officers, or holders of more than 5% of voting securities, along with their family members and affiliates. Committee members with a conflict of interest cannot vote. Certain transactions, like executive compensation, are pre-approved.

sections.title: SECURITY OWNERSHIP OF CERTAIN BENEFICIAL OWNERS AND MANAGEMENT
sections.path: spacex-s1.pdf/SECURITY OWNERSHIP OF CERTAIN BENEFICIAL OWNERS AND MANAGEMENT
sections.summary: The table details beneficial ownership of SpaceX common stock as of May 1, 2026, before and after the offering. It lists major shareholders, including Elon Musk, Gwynne Shotwell, Bret Johnsen, and entities like Valor Equity Partners. Ownership percentages are based on 6.93 billion Class A and 5.60 billion Class B shares outstanding, adjusted for the Class C Reclassification, Preferred Conversion, and 2026 Stock Split. Footnotes explain share holdings, including restricted stock, trusts, options, and voting rights differences between Class A (1 vote per share) and Class B (10 votes per share).

sections.title: DESCRIPTION OF CAPITAL STOCK
sections.summary: The following summary of the Company's capital stock and charter and bylaws (each as in effect upon completion of this offering) does not purport to be complete and is qualified in its entirety by reference to the provisions of applicable law and to our charter and bylaws, which are filed as exhibits to the registration statement of which this prospectus is a part. To understand the material terms of our common stock and preferred stock, you should read our charter and our bylaws in their entirety. For purposes of this section, the term “common stock” refers to our Class A, Class B, and Class C common stock.

children.title: General
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/DESCRIPTION OF CAPITAL STOCK/General
children.summary: Upon completion of this offering, the authorized capital stock of the Company will consist of 36,132,150,000 shares of Class A common stock, par value \$0.001 per share, of which shares will be issued and outstanding, 6,125,000,000 shares of Class B common stock, par value \$0.001 per share, of which shares will be issued and outstanding, 10,000,000,000 shares of Class C common stock, par value \$0.001 per share, of which no shares will be issued and outstanding, and 2,400,000,000 shares of preferred stock, par value \$0.001 per share, of which no shares will be issued and outstanding.

children.title: Common Stock
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/DESCRIPTION OF CAPITAL STOCK/Common Stock

children.title: Voting Rights
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/DESCRIPTION OF CAPITAL STOCK/Common Stock/Voting Rights
children.summary: The passage describes the voting rights of Class A, B, and C common stock. Class A has one vote per share, Class B has ten votes per share, and Class C has no voting rights. Holders of voting common stock generally vote together as a single class. However, Class B stockholders separately elect 51% of directors and must approve removal of Mr. Musk from board and leadership roles. Class B also has separate class votes on charter amendments affecting their rights and on certain mergers or sales.

children.title: Election and Removal of Directors
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/DESCRIPTION OF CAPITAL STOCK/Common Stock/Election and Removal of Directors
children.summary: The charter specifies that Class B common stockholders elect 51% of directors, while all voting common stockholders elect the remaining directors. Class B Directors can be removed or vacancies filled by a majority vote of Class B stockholders. Common Stock Directors can be removed or vacancies filled by a majority vote of all voting common stockholders. Mr. Musk's removal from board and leadership positions requires approval of a majority of Class B stockholders. These voting rights are subject to future preferred stock rights.

children.title: Dividends
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/DESCRIPTION OF CAPITAL STOCK/Common Stock/Dividends
children.summary: Subject to the prior rights of holders of all classes and series of the Company's capital stock at the time outstanding having prior rights as to dividends, the holders of shares of Class A common stock, Class B common stock and Class C common stock will be entitled to receive such dividends as may be declared from time to time by the board. Any dividends paid to the holders of shares of Class A common stock, Class B common stock and Class C common stock will be paid pro rata, on an equal priority, pari passu basis.

children.title: Dissolution and Liquidation
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/DESCRIPTION OF CAPITAL STOCK/Common Stock/Dissolution and Liquidation
children.summary: Upon the Company's liquidation, dissolution or winding up, holders of shares of Class A common stock, Class B common stock and Class C common stock are entitled to share ratably in all assets remaining after payment of liabilities and the liquidation preference of any then outstanding shares of capital stock of the Company.

children.title: Conversion
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/DESCRIPTION OF CAPITAL STOCK/Common Stock/Conversion
children.summary: Class B common stock is convertible into Class A common stock at the holder's option, and automatically converts upon any transfer except for permitted transfers. Permitted transfers include transfers among registered holders, family members, certain entities owned by qualified shareholders, and transfers for charitable or estate planning purposes.

children.title: No Preemptive or Other Rights
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/DESCRIPTION OF CAPITAL STOCK/Common Stock/No Preemptive or Other Rights
children.summary: Holders of the Company's Class A common stock, Class B common stock, and Class C common stock do not have preemptive, subscription, redemption rights, or sinking fund.

children.title: Issuance of Additional Shares
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/DESCRIPTION OF CAPITAL STOCK/Common Stock/Issuance of Additional Shares
children.summary: We may issue additional authorized shares of Class A common stock, Class B common stock and Class C common stock at any time or from time to time, subject to applicable provisions of our charter, our bylaws and Texas law. Our charter will provide that additional shares of Class B common stock may only be issued in the future to Mr. Musk, his family members and certain entities permitted under our charter.

children.title: Preferred Stock
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/DESCRIPTION OF CAPITAL STOCK/Preferred Stock
children.summary: The charter authorizes the board to issue preferred stock without shareholder approval, which could decrease Class A common stock market price. Texas law, charter, and bylaws contain provisions that make acquisitions, proxy contests, and removal of officers/directors more difficult, potentially deterring beneficial transactions but also discouraging coercive takeover practices and encouraging negotiation.

children.title: Anti-takeover statute under Texas law
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/DESCRIPTION OF CAPITAL STOCK/Anti-takeover statute under Texas law
children.summary: We will be subject to Section 21.606 of the TBOC, which in general, prohibits a publicly held Texas corporation, like the Company after the completion of this offering, from engaging, under certain circumstances, in a business combination with an affiliated shareholder (as defined in the TBOC) for a period of three years following the date the person became an affiliated shareholder unless: - the board approved either the business combination or the transaction that resulted in the shareholder becoming an affiliated shareholder before the affiliated shareholder's share acquisition date; or - at or subsequent to the date of the transaction, the business combination is approved by the board and authorized at an annual or special meeting of shareholders, and not by written consent, by the affirmative vote of at least two-thirds of the outstanding voting shares not beneficially owned by the affiliated shareholder or any of its affiliates or associates at a meeting of shareholders called for that purpose not less than six months after the affiliated shareholder's share acquisition date. Provisions of our charter and our bylaws that may have an anti-takeover effect

children.title: Election of Class B Directors
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/DESCRIPTION OF CAPITAL STOCK/Election of Class B Directors
children.summary: The passage outlines key provisions of the company's charter and bylaws, including that Class B common stockholders elect 51% of directors, giving Mr. Musk significant control over board elections and corporate matters. It specifies no cumulative voting, special meetings called by certain officers or 50% shareholders, and shareholder action by written consent with majority votes. Advance notice procedures for shareholder proposals are established, with ownership requirements for nationally listed corporations. Authorized but unissued shares can be issued without shareholder approval, subject to Nasdaq rules for large issuances.

children.title: Corporate Opportunities
children.path: spacex-s1.pdf/DESCRIPTION OF CAPITAL STOCK/Corporate Opportunities
children.summary: The company's charter renounces any interest in business opportunities presented to board members, observers, or attendees (Business Opportunities Exempt Party), even if the company could have pursued them. These parties have no duty to present such opportunities and are indemnified against claims for breach of fiduciary duty, unless the opportunity was presented to them expressly and solely in their capacity as a company director, officer, employee, or shareholder.

children.title: Exclusive Forum and Venue and Arbitration, Jury Trial Waiver
